words: ROB MOORE: Where did you grow up?
HERMIT SCHUPFER: When?

speaker: ROB
words: Where?

speaker: HS
words: At Juliaetta. I was born at Juliaetta, October the 15th, 1892. I Had

speaker: HS
words: a brother that was older, a year and half older than I was and a sister

speaker: HS
words: that was four years younger. We was raised on fathers homestead which

speaker: HS
words: he homesteaded, and after spending five or six years on Droving on it,

speaker: HS
words: he had a deed that was issued by President Arthur in 1884. And ah, his

speaker: HS
words: brother Rupert at the same time homesteaded two quarters of a mile south

speaker: HS
words: and ah, started laying out, and later laid it out as the townsite of

speaker: HS
words: Juliaetta

speaker: ROB
words: Did he lay out the whole 160 as the townsite or just a little bit of

speaker: ROB
words: it as the townsite?

speaker: IIS
words: Ah, it must have been part of it, and I don't exactly have the date...

speaker: ROB
words: That's okay.

speaker: HS
words: And laid it out in the early 90s. And this first post office was

speaker: HS
words: established at this quarter section in between my father's homestead

speaker: HS
words: and Rupert's homestead by Mr. Snyder, and he named this post office

speaker: HS
words: after two of his girls, Julia-Etta, established in 1881. And then

speaker: HS
words: when Rupert laid out the town of Juliaetta...

speaker: ROB
words: It wasn't called Juliaetta when he laid it out though was it?

speaker: HS
words: Ah, laid out there into Juliaetta or anyway when they started to lay

speaker: HS
words: it out. But it incorporated I think in 91 or someplace in there, but

speaker: HS
words: the post office was then moved on to what was called Schupfer and the

speaker: HS
words: name Juliaetta went with it.

speaker: ROB
words: Oh, so that's when the town changed its name from Schupfer to Juliaetta?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, that's when the town changed its name. In the Juliaetta library

speaker: HS
words: there's a article, the signatures of the pnes who changed the name.

speaker: HS
words: Then the school, the first school that I remember, I can. just remember

speaker: HS
words: it being washed out, (was) next to the middle Potlatch where the high

speaker: HS
words: way bridge now is. But the first school that we attended was in 18 ah,

speaker: HS
words: I was five years old that be ah '97, and it stood, it was in the inter

speaker: HS
words: section of the street that runs up from where the depot was. (i could

speaker: HS
words: get those streets now) and in the intersection of those two streets,

speaker: HS
words: right on top of the hill. I remember that we used to coast right out

speaker: HS
words: the—the front steps and get in a washtob or something and slide clear

speaker: HS
words: on down into town. It was a two story building. Burton L. French, ah

speaker: HS
words: I don't remember whether he taught the year we went, but he taught at

speaker: HS
words: that school and that school burnt in 19 and 01 I believe it is, 1900

speaker: HS
words: or 1901, and they had a temporary school down below what was the Grand

speaker: HS
words: Central Hotel-, below main street, until the new school was built where

speaker: HS
words: the present school is now. And the school that was then built burnt

speaker: HS
words: down and the present brick school, primary school, is setting there,

speaker: HS
words: setting on that location. And school, in there was practically sixty

speaker: HS
words: pupils in a room with one teacher. School started at nine o'clock,

speaker: HS
words: recess at ten thirty till ten forty-five, noon at twelve till one, then

speaker: HS
words: ah two thirtjr til quarter to three, to four o'clock. And ah that was

speaker: HS
words: school, teaching the different subjects, no sports included. Any sports

speaker: HS
words: that had was after school on Saturdays or Sundays, (it went to) eighth

speaker: HS
words: grade but we missed some school every year on account of helping farm.

speaker: HS
words: We had to plow with a footburner they called it, with two or three

speaker: HS
words: horses, and we usually had two of them. We'd have to stay out of school

speaker: HS
words: and do the plowing in the fall and also in the spring, and help seed it.

speaker: HS
words: In those days on the farm it was practically lived off what it produced.

speaker: HS
words: Mother had chickens she had about hundred, hundred-fifty chickens and

speaker: HS
words: took the eggs to the store for groceries and things.

speaker: HS
words: Most of the crop

speaker: HS
words: was wheat and corn, and the wheat was generally for hay, sold some of

speaker: HS
words: it, and fed the rest of it to the cattle and the horses. And the off

speaker: HS
words: time in the summertime we'd usually work someplace and try to earn a

speaker: HS
words: few extra nickels. I remember picking potatoes for ten cents a hour,

speaker: HS
words: riding cultivator horse for ten hours a days for two bits, and there

speaker: HS
words: was ah...but when harvest came my brother usually run the binder and

speaker: HS
words: father done the shocking and I dragged the bundles up closer to where

speaker: HS
words: he could set em up,when I was younger. And then at times some of the

speaker: HS
words: summers they had some of the wheat thrashed, and there'd be a horse

speaker: HS
words: power thrashing machine brought down from American Ridge by Martin

speaker: HS
words: Thomas. And later we'd get some of the steam thrashers. Generally

speaker: HS
words: there'd be some shipped in, new ones, and they would thrash ours before

speaker: HS
words: they would take them up on the ridge. And I remember, we used to sell

speaker: HS
words: for five dollars a ton, take it out of the barn ..after it was stored

speaker: HS
words: or out of the stack. We butchered usually a beef and a hog or so in

speaker: HS
words: the fall, and probably sold maybe a front quarter of the beef for five

speaker: HS
words: cents a pound. And ah, our chores when we came home from school, I

speaker: HS
words: got home from school it was practically time to get the cows, and gen

speaker: HS
words: erally they's about as stubborn an animal, they'd be the furthest away

speaker: HS
words: on the place, and that would be three quarters of a mile. I went to

speaker: HS
words: go get the cows and bring them in and milk em. And first mother had

speaker: HS
words: the milk in pans in the basement and let the cream settle on top, til

speaker: HS
words: the cream separator came into use and we got a small cream separator

speaker: HS
words: and cream station, Haselwood Cream Station at Juliaetta, run by Mr.

speaker: HS
words: Kennedy was operated for him, and we'd take our cream through the

speaker: HS
words: separator and take it down to the cream station. And later they had

speaker: HS
words: a truck go through and pick up the cream and ah...

speaker: ROB
words: The cream station was at the railroad? It was shipped out on the

speaker: ROB
words: railroad?

speaker: HS
words: The what?

speaker: ROB
words: The cream would be shipped out on the railroad?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, the cream was shipped to Spokane, it would be pasteurized there

speaker: HS
words: at Juliaetta and shipped out to Spokane on the railroad. And when my

speaker: HS
words: mother had more eggs than the store demanded they used to send to Spokane

speaker: HS
words: by the cases, and get I think about ten or fifteen cents a dozen. And

speaker: HS
words: ah, as boys, different farms, small farms around there, after school

speaker: HS
words: was out and Saturdays we were busy, but we usually had Sunday to our

speaker: HS
words: selves quite a little, and mostly in the springtime we used to go out

speaker: HS
words: and shoot squirrels. And ah, we did have a ball team at (Juliaetta),

speaker: HS
words: we couldn't take time off enough to play with the school teams so we

speaker: HS
words: had what they called a scrub team, and we usually played the school

speaker: HS
words: team on Sundays but we generally could beat em. But ah, that was the

speaker: HS
words: sports. If we'd go to Kendrick to play the team up there, we'd walk up

speaker: HS
words: to Kendrick and play, and ah maybe on the way home hit for the swimming

speaker: HS
words: hole and then walk the rest of the way home. And as we grew a little

speaker: HS
words: older, ah the tramway warehouse and the tramway was just opposite

speaker: HS
words: across over on Potlatch Ridge from our place. We used to go over there.

speaker: HS
words: It was owned by a Mr. E. W. Porter and Lawrence Porter's part interest.

speaker: HS
words: He was manager tlsre but we chummed a lot with his son and we'd go over

speaker: HS
words: to the warehouse and eh, generally happen, ah roll down some sacks off

speaker: HS
words: of the pile if they were shipping out, or we'd ah get em some drinking

speaker: HS
words: water and do a few chores, and maybe sometimes we'd get a nickel and

speaker: HS
words: sometimes we wouldn't. And ah we'd sa.w wood for the boiler that they

speaker: HS
words: had, A steam boiler there to run the steam engines when -ohey were

speaker: HS
words: cleaning or tramming over across to the railroad track with the bucket

speaker: HS
words: trams, and we used to saw the cord wood into three pieces so that they'd

speaker: HS
words: go into the boiler. (We'd) help a cousin of mine, Joe Schaipfer, was

speaker: HS
words: generally fireman. And then they had a telephone line going up the hill

speaker: HS
words: to the upper warehouse and to the bank owned by the same people that

speaker: HS
words: had the tramway, and ah we'd help 'em fix up the phone line if it got

speaker: HS
words: down, sometime move the phone from one end to other, and to get in later

speaker: HS
words: they changed to gasoline engines. Ue had gotten the gasoline engine in

speaker: HS
words: 1908 and knew a little about the engine. And they bought a Fairbanks-

speaker: HS
words: Horse engine. Fellows didn't know anything about the engine so we got

speaker: HS
words: ten cents an hour for either one of us if we'd tend to that engine at

speaker: HS
words: the warehouse, and that was our first job.

speaker: ROB
words: The engine helped power the tram?

speaker: HS
words: It was a two horse power, two horse power engine, and I remember they

speaker: HS
words: used deisel oil and the tanks were a certain distance away from the

speaker: HS
words: warehouse, it had to be kind of pumped in. It got started with a large

speaker: HS
words: watertank on it. We'd help there, roll sacks down over and also we'd

speaker: HS
words: help Porter with his telephone system. It was R. H. Porter, he was a

speaker: HS
words: brother of E. W. or uncle of this boy we chummed with, and the first...

speaker: HS
words: ah there was some spare telephone wire in the warehouse and they give

speaker: HS
words: us, Mr. Porter said we could have that, so we string it between our

speaker: HS
words: houses and got the ringers out of a couple of telephone sets, old

speaker: HS
words: ringers that wasn't in use, and made us a telegraph set. We used that

speaker: HS
words: for awhile. Ue extended it to another place or two but it was rather

speaker: HS
words: slow going. And ah we got in contact with R. H. Porter and the ware

speaker: HS
words: house and we rustled enough pieces to make a couple of telephones.

speaker: HS
words: Ue put the telephones together ah in an old plug-cut tobbacco box and

speaker: HS
words: then we used that telephone. And that started us up. Then it seemed

speaker: HS
words: like Porter had started his telephone system up on the ridges. The

speaker: HS
words: first fellow, fellow by the name of Fredrickson back of the upper

speaker: HS
words: tramway warehouse, they hooked him up onto this line. And Mr. R. H.

speaker: HS
words: Porter, he had came from back East, he brought a telephone with him

speaker: HS
words: and he hooked it on and then the other farmers wanted service so he

speaker: HS
words: got a switch board and some telephones and used the top barbwire for

speaker: HS
words: the telephone line and run telephones all over the ridges. We would

speaker: HS
words: help him, got to helping him part time on and off. First I remember we

speaker: HS
words: got five dollars a week and our dinner, and later we worked for twenty

speaker: HS
words: cents an hour for him, and did whatever was to be done on and off until

speaker: HS
words: he took sick and we bought it from him in 1915. In the meantime also

speaker: HS
words: in the fall I worked at the cannery canning tomatoes.

speaker: ROB
words: The cannery at Juliaetta?

speaker: HS
words: At Juliaetta, there was a large community cannery there. All the

speaker: HS
words: available cultivated land was rented for tomatoes, and also the site

speaker: HS
words: at Lapwai where Spaulding had his mill, and they still used that flume

speaker: HS
words: to irrigate tomatoes on that flat down there that he had. The freight

speaker: HS
words: train would bring them up to the cannery every evening. And ah we canned

speaker: HS
words: over record can was ah a common size tomato can, and we canned ten

speaker: HS
words: thousand cans on a record day. So I worked there for a month and on

speaker: HS
words: through later on and I also canned some peaches and some prunes,

speaker: ROB
words: What was the brand name these were canned under?

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: What was the brand name of the cans?

speaker: IIS
words: What Brand?

speaker: ROB
words: Yes.

speaker: HS
words: Well these cans are put out, mostly in tomatoes, three brands: we

speaker: HS
words: call them the number one and that was a Bridge brand; and number two

speaker: HS
words: was next grade was average, good average grade was mostly most of them

speaker: HS
words: was and they called them the Seaport brand, that was the pieces left

speaker: HS
words: over, but this second brand most of it came under different brands a

speaker: HS
words: lot of it came under Royal Club. They would send the labels, so many

speaker: HS
words: labels, and they'd label so many cans, and usually ship out a car load

speaker: HS
words: of them to Mason. I believe it was Lewiston Merchantile that handled

speaker: HS
words: them. And ah then there was a Clarkston brand, there was a Juliaetta

speaker: HS
words: brand, and different ones. It was interesting. For instance one time

speaker: HS
words: I was at the place, and they sent the boy to the store to get two cans

speaker: HS
words: of Royal Club Tomatoes. And I says to the lady "Vhy don't you buy two

speaker: HS
words: cans of Juliaetta brand and you get the same thing and you get them

speaker: HS
words: for two bits, two for a quarter instead of thirty cents." "Oh" she

speaker: HS
words: said "no" she said "they don't compare" so I told her I said "now I

speaker: HS
words: done run the capper and those cans were all marked" and asked her if

speaker: HS
words: she would believe me if we get a can of each and take the label off

speaker: HS
words: and see if they didn't have the same mark. And they did. we11, after

speaker: HS
words: that she wojjdrft buy any Royal Club tomatoes (laugh laugh) so imagination

speaker: HS
words: is quite a thing. But she told them how much better they were than the

speaker: HS
words: Juliaetta brand tomatoes we© but ah ah

speaker: ROB
words: Did the cannery contract with the tomato growers, or what?

speaker: HS
words: They contracted these tomatoes, Now we had, on our home place they

speaker: HS
words: rented the ground, but other places they generally paid, at ah first

speaker: HS
words: a little more, but I believe I wouldn't say this too accurate, but pay

speaker: HS
words: about twenty dollars a ton. But you»d be surprised how many tomatoes

speaker: HS
words: went on that ground. And after the tomatoes had froze and the canning

speaker: HS
words: was over, generally my brother and I would go on to our place and pull

speaker: HS
words: the vines and shake the tomatoes off them and pile them up and bum

speaker: HS
words: the vines. And then we would just harrow orspringthooth this ground,

speaker: HS
words: and to walk behind of it it was just sop sop sop going Into the tomatoes.

speaker: HS
words: And ah they raised them, they bought the tomatoes and rented what ground

speaker: HS
words: they could get then, anyway to get the tomatoes. Now that cannery was

speaker: HS
words: run, I don't remember just when it started, but It was running about

speaker: HS
words: 1915 and 16. I believe it run in 191?. I believe it burned up, it

speaker: HS
words: burnt in 1917 or 18 in there. It was destroyed by fire.

speaker: HS
words: And that

speaker: HS
words: cannery was on the ground where first was a ball diamond close to It

speaker: HS
words: and a park and a warehouse, and then at the present time the Gem State

speaker: HS
words: Lumber Company is on on that property. And there used to be, all the

speaker: HS
words: wheat was handled in sacks, and first it was hauled to these warehouses,

speaker: HS
words: tramway warehouses, and this Juliaetta tramway was a three rail car

speaker: HS
words: affair, one would go up and the other one down, halfway in the middle

speaker: HS
words: the middle one would split and it would have four rails so they could

speaker: HS
words: pass, with a steam engine on top. Take down about fifty sacks of wheat,

speaker: HS
words: a little more of oa.ts and other. In the fall there was a lot of apples

speaker: HS
words: raised on up on the ridges and the apples would be trammed down, and

speaker: HS
words: in cherry season they would pack cherries in the warehouses and tram

speaker: HS
words: the cherries down and load them and ship them up by the car loads.

speaker: HS
words: There was a lot of fruit raised and shipped out at that time. And

speaker: HS
words: prunes, there was prune dryers. There was, for instance American Ridge

speaker: HS
words: up there, there was a Hutchinson prune dryer, and the Johnson prune

speaker: HS
words: dryer, and the Russell prune dryer. There was three prune dryers

speaker: HS
words: right here in one neighborhood with each one with a prune orchard.

speaker: HS
words: With a large prune orchard.

speaker: HS
words: And up on Potlatch Ridge there was a

speaker: HS
words: prune orchard, and prunes was quite a thing. And at home, back to

speaker: HS
words: when they was homesteading, my father came over from Austria. He had

speaker: HS
words: a half brother in Missouri sometime in the 1860's, and he came to

speaker: HS
words: Missouri where this half brother was and then did carpenter work.

speaker: HS
words: And then he traveled around with carpenter work and his brother Rupert

speaker: HS
words: came and I don't know where they met, but anyway father carpentered

speaker: HS
words: Los Angles and San Francisco and their were together at Portland and

speaker: HS
words: they was looking for a place to homestead, and said that they looked

speaker: HS
words: at a place there and they was kind of interested, but it needed a lot

speaker: HS
words: of clearing. So they cleared off a piece and figured out how long it

speaker: HS
words: would take them to clear it, and they figured it a little more and that

speaker: HS
words: wasn't the thing. So they came further in and towards Lewiston. My

speaker: HS
words: uncle said he came through by way of Yakima, and I thinks my father done

speaker: HS
words: carpentry work along and Rupert was scouting around to see the location.

speaker: HS
words: He says that he traveled with Indians. He'd get an Indian to get him

speaker: HS
words: a saddle horse and go with him, and hire him to go to a certain place.

speaker: HS
words: But he said he never...he always had it laid out that he'd always stop

speaker: HS
words: at a stopping-place before where he'd had him hired. He said he just

speaker: HS
words: didn't trust them too much, and they'd get to where he could get another

speaker: HS
words: one to take him again. He didn't have one for too long, and he didn't

speaker: HS
words: use one for the whole journey. He says he came up, I think he was

speaker: HS
words: alone but I'm not sure, he came up through Umatilla when General Howard

speaker: HS
words: was going down with his army in 1877. He got into Lewiston and he and

speaker: HS
words: father then went up into Anatone country and they was in a cabin up

speaker: HS
words: there for a few days and ah...turn it off?

speaker: ROB
words: No its okay.

speaker: HS
words: ...ah then they decided to come out of there and they went back to

speaker: HS
words: Lewiston. Father done carpenter work but I think it was up in Colton,

speaker: HS
words: Genesee country. A fellow by the name of Moxley at Lewiston told Rupert

speaker: HS
words: that... advise him to go up to Potlatch Creek up through Genesee, the

speaker: HS
words: old town of Genesee was in a different location than the present town

speaker: HS
words: is, but not very far out, and to go over Fix'Ridge, and down there at

speaker: HS
words: the junction where the Middle Potlatch runs into the Big Potlatch that's

speaker: HS
words: his last location in there, and they figured there'd be a railroad come

speaker: HS
words: through. So he went up and -rent as far as Genesee, up Fix Ridge and he

speaker: HS
words: looked over and it didn't look too good to him. He went back again,

speaker: HS
words: but as time went by again he thought he'd just go up there and go down

speaker: HS
words: and just really see what it looked like. And he went on down and he

speaker: HS
words: found a lot of nice bunch grass, and good soil, prairie chickens, fish

speaker: HS
words: and good fishing, and the salmon in the creek and the Potlatch there,

speaker: HS
words: and so they decided to homestead there. So they picked the two places

speaker: HS
words: they had, one quarter a mile between them. Father's homestead was a

speaker: HS
words: quarter mile wide and a mile long, and he had a cabin on his and Rupert

speaker: HS
words: had a cabin on his. Near as I get it they had to sleep on their place

speaker: HS
words: a week out of each year and had to be so much improvements made, so

speaker: HS
words: Rupert done the improving. He planted an orchard and done some fencing

speaker: HS
words: on both the places while father went out and rustled the money. They

speaker: HS
words: also took up a preemption of 160 acres someway, I don't know how that

speaker: HS
words: worked. Whether they had to pay a little for some of it or something,

speaker: HS
words: on Potlatch Ridge. Which is now owned by Doctor Moser and farmed by

speaker: HS
words: ah Fred Glen. But in 1888 father went back to Europe and then mother

speaker: HS
words: came with him and they were married in New York.

speaker: HS
words: (End pf Side A.

speaker: HS
words: It was never mentioned one of em, but mother said that the reason they

speaker: HS
words: left Anatone. They had a cabin there and they ran out of food. And

speaker: HS
words: Rupert he happened to find apig someplace, and he got it so they had

speaker: HS
words: some food. And then the next day or so a stranger came along, and said

speaker: HS
words: in those days you took anyone if they wanted to stay you took them in,

speaker: HS
words: so he stayed with them over night. And during their conversation he

speaker: HS
words: mentioned about that he heard over town, over at I guess there wasn't

speaker: HS
words: much town but anyway there, that he heard them talking about they figured

speaker: HS
words: there was going to be ahanging there in aday or so. They didn't say

speaker: HS
words: who, but anyway, they didn't know whether this fellow came to scare them

speaker: HS
words: out so he could get this place, or whether it was actually so, but they

speaker: HS
words: didn'ttake any chances and they pulled out of Anatone and went back to

speaker: HS
words: Lewiston. The only thing Rupert ever told me, I asked him one time if

speaker: HS
words: he ever got in trouble with the Indians over hy Anatone or anything.

speaker: HS
words: Well, he said had a little trouble one time. He said he was riding a

speaker: HS
words: saddle horse down and one of the Indian horses followed him, and the

speaker: HS
words: Indians started to get rough. And I told my mother about what he had

speaker: HS
words: told me, and she said "Yes, and I bet there was a rope "between him and

speaker: HS
words: that horse" (laugh laugh) so...

speaker: ROB
words: Rupert sounds like he was quite a character.

speaker: HS
words: Yes. Well I guess those days you done the best to get by with. I

speaker: HS
words: think they went back to Lewiston and then...did I mention about Genesee,

speaker: HS
words: coming down?

speaker: ROB
words: Yes.

speaker: HS
words: I've mentioned that have I?

speaker: ROB
words: You've gotten to the point where they've...

speaker: IIS
words: Yes, this is before they met Moxley and he told them to come up through

speaker: IIS
words: Genesee and go down there and look that over, and he looked it over and

speaker: IIS
words: found the good bunch grass, and prairie chickens, and salmon in the Pot

speaker: IIS
words: latch creek there. And so they homesteaded there. And ah Mr. Stranahar,

speaker: IIS
words: I've remembered him at different times at the fourth of July celebrations

speaker: IIS
words: or something, he'd be the speaker. He was one of the first settlers on

speaker: IIS
words: American Ridge and he says when the Schupfers homesteaded down there

speaker: IIS
words: they felt sorry for em, that they was gonna have to haul all their pro

speaker: IIS
words: duce up over the ridges to go to market. But he says it turned out the

speaker: IIS
words: other way, that the railroad come through (down below) and they had to

speaker: IIS
words: haul theirs down there. So it turned out all right. And this preemption

speaker: IIS
words: I was telling about awhile ago that father took up. When mother came

speaker: IIS
words: they sold, that for two thousand dollars, and that give em some money to

speaker: IIS
words: help build up the home there and one thing and another. They built it

speaker: IIS
words: there. Mother said first there was very few people there. There was

speaker: IIS
words: another uncle, Florian Schupfer, came. A brother of Rupert's and father's.

speaker: IIS
words: He settled there and I believe it was 1880, ah I wasn't so sure I think

speaker: IIS
words: it was 1883. But I'm not sure of the date.

speaker: IIS
words: He came there and he home

speaker: IIS
words: steaded there at Juliaetta and most of the visiting or associates until

speaker: IIS
words: we went to school was with them. Another fellow named there Matt Walcher,

speaker: IIS
words: he bought a place there. Bachelor he was there, and Mother said that

speaker: IIS
words: they had apples, orchards that they planted before got to producing

speaker: IIS
words: apples, and the Indian women would usually come on Sundays to buy apples

speaker: IIS
words: and would always stay and visit. Said that they'd sit around and have

speaker: IIS
words: good visit, but then one couldn't talk the other's language, but said

speaker: IIS
words: there was usually twp or three of them and they had a good time anyway.

speaker: IIS
words: They would pay. They always got along good withjthem and sometimes they

speaker: IIS
words: say they'd pay later and sometimes they were very trustful as far as the

speaker: IIS
words: Indians was concerned in the early times. And mother said there was one

speaker: IIS
words: fellow there that kept getting apples, and he says "I just don't have

speaker: IIS
words: the money but I'll pay yon" and "I'll pay you" and they give him. They

speaker: IIS
words: felt like they should turn him down, but thought maybe they would cause

speaker: IIS
words: some friction. They was kind of careful about It because there was kind

speaker: IIS
words: of different incidents. But one day he comes, he was just all smiles

speaker: IIS
words: and he wanted them to come outside and take a look, and she said he had

speaker: IIS
words: a great big salmon hanging up there. And he told them "I'll pay you.

speaker: IIS
words: I'll pay you." (laugh laugh) So she said that was well paid, she said

speaker: IIS
words: that was.

speaker: IIS
words: And talk about salmon, I can remember there was a railroad

speaker: IIS
words: bridge across the Potlatch there and I can remember seeing my uncle and

speaker: IIS
words: different ones on there with spears, and they'd spear the salmon as

speaker: IIS
words: they went through under. I was up at Coeur" d' Alene here a number of years ago,

speaker: IIS
words: getting some information about earlier times down here. The people that

speaker: IIS
words: run a dairy between here and Juliaetta, this fellow's name was Burnes,

speaker: IIS
words: and so stopped at Coeur d' .Alene and talked with him. He had some

speaker: IIS
words: pictures of down there but none of them that showed anything much, but

speaker: IIS
words: the thing that he said that he really remembers from down there is they

speaker: IIS
words: had a sawmill there, and they had the Potlatch Creek dammed. And on

speaker: IIS
words: Sundays generally his father would, I don't know whether they said open

speaker: IIS
words: the gates on the dam and let the dam dry up or else close the gates to

speaker: IIS
words: let it da?y out below, but anyway he remembers there was usually about

speaker: IIS
words: forty or fifty people be there waiting until it got dry and the fish

speaker: IIS
words: start to flop. And he says he just remembers an Indian just peeling all

speaker: IIS
words: his clothes off and jumping right in that creek and grabbing the fish

speaker: IIS
words: and getting them out of there. He says that part he can remember. But

speaker: IIS
words: he said he went in there with all those people there without a stitch

speaker: IIS
words: on but he was going to get those fish, (chuckle)

speaker: ROB
words: At that time were the Indians still moving pretty freely through this

speaker: ROB
words: part of the countrv?

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: At that time were the Indians still moving pretty freely through this

speaker: ROB
words: part of the country?

speaker: HS
words: No they was pretty well settled on a reservation down below Juliaetta.

speaker: HS
words: Like Mox Mox, some of those fellows, I think Waters, they was pretty

speaker: HS
words: well settled and actually they was just a dandy bunch of Indians and

speaker: HS
words: they always are and to my notion they still are. I can remember that

speaker: HS
words: father had troubles some way with the horses, they run away or something,

speaker: HS
words: and there was a certain place and I don't know how many people were

speaker: HS
words: standing on the side walk watching my father trying to get things tangledout.

speaker: HS
words: This Indian went out and helped him. They was just alright. But

speaker: HS
words: there was sometimes, there was a brewery there in town and where they

speaker: HS
words: made beer, and in this temporary school when the other one burnt, there

speaker: HS
words: was an upstairs and a downstairs in the building with kind of an over

speaker: HS
words: head porch from the top to get out in front towards the street and

speaker: HS
words: then go down the side steps. And anyway during the noon hour there

speaker: HS
words: was an Indian came up from this brewery and he was just drunk as he

speaker: HS
words: could be. Some of the girls started to holler Indian language to him

speaker: HS
words: and pretty soon he drew out a knife and he started to cross the street

speaker: HS
words: towards the school. I know I for one of them jumped out the upstairs

speaker: HS
words: window. I bumped my nose, I had a nosebleed, but I went up around and

speaker: HS
words: we all peeked around the corner up above. But some of the bigger boys

speaker: HS
words: they met the Indian and talked to him and he went on the other way again,

speaker: HS
words: so everything quieted down but ah...(laugh). There vras things like that

speaker: HS
words: to talk about. To mention about trouble with ah horses, runaway was

speaker: HS
words: a very common thing those days. You'd be in the store someplace or

speaker: HS
words: something and the first thing you know the people hollering and there'd

speaker: HS
words: be a team go down through, tore lose and run away, and then when auto

speaker: HS
words: mobiles came why that made it worse than ever. The train was bad enough.

speaker: HS
words: It seemed like, I know my father had to have it and I think all of them

speaker: HS
words: did, going from Juliaetta to Kendrick you could parallel with the rail

speaker: HS
words: road, and whenever a train came they always start to get their horses

speaker: HS
words: to running, sorta speed em up. So they got in the habit when they saw

speaker: HS
words: a train coming they figured they had to run, and it got so they couldn't

speaker: HS
words: hold em and then they would have a runaway, I know father fixed his

speaker: HS
words: hack that he could unhook a lever on the inside and the singletrees

speaker: HS
words: would tip and and unhook the tugs and the horses could go on with the

speaker: HS
words: neck yoke, (laugh laugh) So runaway was a common thing and ah,

speaker: ROB
words: If there was a runaway in town or something would people try to stop

speaker: ROB
words: the horses or let them run right through?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, they try to stop them if they had a chance, but generally they'd

speaker: HS
words: get someplace where they get tangled up with something, one part of them

speaker: HS
words: try to go one way and one the other or something and get out in the brush

speaker: HS
words: or someplace. The first Sunday school, they called this Lady Grandma

speaker: HS
words: Pierce, she was one of the oldtimers there, she said she taught the

speaker: HS
words: first Sunday school down on what was the Aldrich place, which is down

speaker: HS
words: a little ways below Juliaetta, was really the first one. Then the Pre

speaker: HS
words: sbyterian had a church in town and later this bought by the Lutherans.

speaker: HS
words: My folks, mother was a Lutheran, and we always went to church every

speaker: HS
words: other Sunday. That's where my brother and I were confirmed and learned

speaker: HS
words: what German language as far as reading was concerned, and also more or

speaker: HS
words: less speaking the high German because ah my folks and all we spoke the

speaker: HS
words: Austrian dialect. when I first went to school there was very little

speaker: HS
words: English that either one of us knew, and I learned it at school. As

speaker: HS
words: time went on, I can remember one time I was out in the field and I was

speaker: HS
words: a small fellow, and a fellow had asked me to go over to the house and

speaker: HS
words: ask the lady the time. When I got to the house I couldn't remember whether

speaker: HS
words: it was what time is it, or what o'clock is it, but I asked the lady if

speaker: HS
words: she would tell me what o'clock it was. (laugh laugh) Well she knew

speaker: HS
words: what I meant, she was a neighbor woman there. We farmed there, and

speaker: HS
words: worked for the telephone company in the 1915, and also the local light

speaker: HS
words: plant. There was a flour mill'there, water power, and they installed

speaker: HS
words: in 1903 a generator and served the town with electric power...

speaker: ROB
words: Off the creek, off the water power?

speaker: HS
words: Water power, umhuh, the mill was water power and a little bit later on

speaker: HS
words: it served from time it got dark until about midnight. But electric iron

speaker: HS
words: started to come in use then they had what they call the electric ironing

speaker: HS
words: morning, and they'd have the power ontil about noon, usually on Wednesdays,

speaker: HS
words: All of these towns around here had about the same thing. So we worked,

speaker: HS
words: we done the line work practically for those people, and then worked for

speaker: HS
words: Porter. I think if I remember right, besides Porter my brother and I

speaker: HS
words: was the only ones that did climb poles around there. If there was any

speaker: HS
words: pole climbing we did that, and so about 1915 Mr. Porter got sick. He

speaker: HS
words: had to quit on account of" his health and brother and I, we bought it.

speaker: HS
words: With his brother being a banker he loaned us the money and ah...

speaker: ROB
words: Porter's brother was a banker?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, the two Porters. One had the telephone and the other had the

speaker: HS
words: warehouse and the bank. Porter had a partner hy the name of Laurence, but

speaker: HS
words: later Porter had it by himself, and I don't know just when the date was

speaker: HS
words: that it changed. Up until 1915 in there, for quite a number of years

speaker: HS
words: we'd run that tramway in the fall, and we'd have charge of the warehouse

speaker: HS
words: in the wintertime, and load the wheat out, or the grain. It would be

speaker: HS
words: trammed down from the warehouse on top which was not a very large ware

speaker: HS
words: house, I believe it was about fifty feet long and then I don't remember

speaker: HS
words: how wide, the lower warehouse was two hundred feet long and I believe

speaker: HS
words: it was fifty five feet wide, and then there was cable buckets to take

speaker: HS
words: one sack apiece over to the railroad tracks and load it across Potlatch

speaker: HS
words: Creek and load it in the cars.

speaker: ROB
words: What powered the cable?

speaker: HS
words: We used a gasoline engine on that. We used a gasoline engine. At first

speaker: HS
words: the railroad went over there but they couldn't have a high bridge, high

speaker: HS
words: water would wash it out. Then they put in this cable tram and that way

speaker: HS
words: usually two of our country boys there and brother and I, the four of us

speaker: HS
words: we'd usually load the cars out through the winter and give us a little

speaker: HS
words: extra in the winter time. Then on Sundays in the winter when there's

speaker: HS
words: not much to do, we'd go over in. the warehouse and roller skate, (laugh)

speaker: HS
words: Pass the time that way. My brother worked in the garage business.

speaker: HS
words: Automobiles started to come. Motorcycle. First we had a bicycle and

speaker: HS
words: we made attachments for that, copied it out of a Sears and Roebuck

speaker: HS
words: catalogue so to hold it on the railroad rails. We used to ride it on

speaker: HS
words: the railroad rails and then later we got a motorcycle. Used that on going

speaker: HS
words: out on some trouble work instead of a team. Then my brother worked in

speaker: HS
words: a garage in Eendrick for Guy Lewis, and in 1915 then Ihey ordered a

speaker: HS
words: car load of Maxwell automobiles. we took one of them and had a car, and

speaker: HS
words: just about...soon after we had that why this telephone proposition come

speaker: HS
words: up, so we had to borrow all the money for that, and we had it. There

speaker: HS
words: was two telephone offices here at that time, two systems and most of

speaker: HS
words: the stores had both telephones. On the ridges there was about half of

speaker: HS
words: the people couldn't talk to the other half because one went to one

speaker: HS
words: central office and the other went to the other. But ah in 1915 soon

speaker: HS
words: after we bought it, the old Interstate Telephone Company decided to (a

speaker: HS
words: Spokane concern) to quit this country here and we took over Kendrick

speaker: HS
words: and Troy was taken over at Moscow.

speaker: HS
words: Then Juliaetta had their own system

speaker: HS
words: down there, too, that connected with" them. There was two systems down

speaker: HS
words: therethey combined with our system to get It all in one system. And

speaker: HS
words: also later the different switch boards up on the ridges at Southwick,

speaker: HS
words: Leland ., Cameron, Cavendish, TLkon and out in farm places there was

speaker: HS
words: generally a switch and never could tell how far they could get by call

speaker: HS
words: ing from one switch on to the other one. when they couldn't hear, why

speaker: HS
words: they had whoever had done the switching repeat for them and they'd

speaker: HS
words: usually get a message someplace, somewhere. But after that quit and

speaker: HS
words: with this Potlatch Telephone Company which we had, we had connection

speaker: HS
words: with the Bell people for long distance out. I took charge of it until

speaker: HS
words: the First World War and I was over in Seattle at Bremerton, worked in

speaker: HS
words: a machine shop at Bremerton til the end of the war while my brother took

speaker: HS
words: care of the telephone system then here. Up to that time I took care of

speaker: HS
words: it, and as I come back I was free. I helped when they sold a car at

speaker: HS
words: the garage I would go out and stay with these people for a couple, three

speaker: HS
words: days to teach them how to drive. Till in the fall of 1919 Ed Deobald and

speaker: HS
words: I bought out Lewis of this Kendrick garage and we operated it over next

speaker: HS
words: to where the theatre is now. After two years, a little over two years, I

speaker: HS
words: sold out to Ed's brother Brian. Then later they bought and served out of

speaker: HS
words: that, and they put in a bulk plant and delivered gas and had a good business

speaker: HS
words: there. Ed still owns it. Ed Deobald is still there at times and it's run

speaker: HS
words: by two of his boys are now running it. I then run a truck and hauled lum

speaker: HS
words: ber off of Cedar Creek, and between times beans and wheat for the farmers,

speaker: HS
words: with a two ton Garford truck with soled tires. Over the old Cedar Creek

speaker: HS
words: grade, that was a bad one up there. Spent one summer that way. The lumber,

speaker: HS
words: I remember, I made two trips a day, just pick it up board by board and put

speaker: HS
words: it on the truck, and if I remember right hauled right around three thousand

speaker: HS
words: feet. Then I'd go down and push it in a box car and load it in the hot day.

speaker: HS
words: The fellow, Fred Crocker, that I was hauling for said he got I believe it

speaker: HS
words: was 17 or 18 dollars a thousand for that lumber in the car in those days.

speaker: ROB

speaker: HS
words: Well, after I sold out I went to...ah mother and I went to California.

speaker: HS
words: The fellow that I knew, telephone man out of Spokane, had bought the

speaker: HS
words: telephone system in Huntington Beach, and as mother and I went down

speaker: HS
words: for the winter I went up to see this fellow. He talked me into the

speaker: HS
words: notion go buying a pair of climbers and a belt and go to work. So I

speaker: HS
words: told him I would after I bummed around for two weeks. I went to work

speaker: HS
words: for him that winter and in the spring he offered me a good deal if I

speaker: HS
words: would stay over the summer, and so I stayed, over the summer. We run

speaker: HS
words: cable out into the oil fields, the oil fields was starting then, and

speaker: HS
words: took care of the country phones out of (there are different places

speaker: HS
words: that had a switch board) Smeltzer I believe the place was called, and

speaker: HS
words: there was two or three little stations out of those towns. Took care

speaker: HS
words: of that and then the next winter he persuaded me, of course I figured

speaker: HS
words: on if it got winter I'd stay anyway, but went down to Laguna Beach,

speaker: HS
words: There was only a toll phone down there, and two more fellows that had

speaker: HS
words: never had any experience, ah. hired them and we put up lines in Laguna

speaker: HS
words: Beach and we installed a switch board.

speaker: HS
words: We had about fifty phones on

speaker: HS
words: there by the time I left. And mother decided she'd like better come up

speaker: HS
words: here, there's more of her friends, so we came back up here a little

speaker: HS
words: undecided what to do. While I was here a fellow that owned, that started

speaker: HS
words: the Potlatch Electric Company in Kendrick and had it, he persuaded me,

speaker: HS
words: I'd worked for him before helped him with line work, he persuaded me

speaker: HS
words: to help him out for awhile, and which I did. Then after awhile he

speaker: HS
words: told me, he says that fellow that's working for him wants to go some

speaker: HS
words: place else because he couldn't do any line work. He was a graduate

speaker: HS
words: and knew his stuff but he couldn't get up the pole. He just couldn't

speaker: HS
words: make it. And he wanted me to take it and so I told him I'd take it

speaker: HS
words: temporarily. But anyway he went on and I worked for him then. And

speaker: HS
words: in 192? they decided to build up to Bovill and I had charge of a crew

speaker: HS
words: of fifteen men there with none of them linemen, all green men. We would

speaker: HS
words: build a line up into Bovill and nut power in there. And just the time

speaker: HS
words: we got near Bovill it was sold to Washington Water Power Company and

speaker: HS
words: so I was with the local representative here at Kendrick, taking care

speaker: HS
words: of Kendrick, Juliaetta, and Troy first when Wilmot had it. Then built

speaker: HS
words: into Bovill. After that we come down. here, come back here...Lets back

speaker: HS
words: up a little..,it sold in 1927 to the Washington Power Company,

speaker: ROB
words: The man who owned it before that was named Wilmot?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, Wilmot had this Potlatch Electric Company that was started in

speaker: HS
words: 1915, and I worked for him before, and then I worked for him, but when

speaker: HS
words: he sold to the Washington water Company they started and made a District

speaker: HS
words: office in Kendrick. Served Juliaetta, Bovill, Deary, and Troy out of

speaker: HS
words: the office here while before when Wilmot had it until they built this

speaker: HS
words: line there was Troy, Juliaetta and Kendrick served out of here, out

speaker: HS
words: of this office. Then they put in a local office at Bovill. When we

speaker: HS
words: got through in. the fall of after it was sold, why most was local men,

speaker: HS
words: they were through except about three or four, three or two of us, and

words: we were transferred down to ah Kendrick. Come back to Kendrick, brought

speaker: HS
words: our line truck down, which is the first one that that electric company

words: owned, Potlatch Electric Company. It was an old model T Ford with a

speaker: HS
words: Euxtel axle in it, and the roads was dirt.

speaker: HS
words: (End of Side B)

speaker: HS
words: When I first started to work for the telephone, this Carl Porter, the

speaker: HS
words: nephew of R. H. Porter, and I was sent up here to Kendrick to take a

speaker: HS
words: telephone out of the saloon, and take it up about six or seven blocks

speaker: HS
words: on the hillside and install it in the residence. We had one horse and

speaker: HS
words: buggy. We came and got the telephone and put it in the back of the

speaker: HS
words: buggy and drove up on the hill up there. And we were stringing our

speaker: HS
words: wire to the house and thin fellow, the saloon keeper , whose name was

speaker: HS
words: Terry, he was a rather heavy set fellow. He come up that hill and he

speaker: HS
words: was just all out of breath and just looked awful worried and just about

speaker: HS
words: scared us to death. We just wondered what we did down there that we

speaker: HS
words: were going to catch it. And he come up and he wanted to know where we

speaker: HS
words: had that telephone. We told him it was back in the buggy. He didn't

speaker: HS
words: say a word and he went there and opened the battery box and took out his

speaker: HS
words: daily receipts. (laugh laugh) So we noticed he felt a lot better and

speaker: HS
words: I know we sure did. We sure was relieved.

speaker: HS
words: Another time, there those

speaker: HS
words: days there wasn't too many people who'd have a phone. There'd be one

speaker: HS
words: phone in a certain block and it would get so the neighbors would go and

speaker: HS
words: visit and want to use the phone. Then the person who had the phone would

speaker: HS
words: want us to stop these calls, but they didn't want to be involved. They

speaker: HS
words: didn't wanta, they beins friends. Which then was a five cent charge nut

speaker: HS
words: on all the calls. And then the operator would call back after the people

speaker: HS
words: hung up and tell them that was a .five cent call. But I don't think as

speaker: HS
words: far as the company, we never collected them just let the people have

speaker: HS
words: them. But some of em, they griped about that and they wasn't going to

speaker: HS
words: pay the five cents, and some of the business people. But the one that

speaker: HS
words: I always think about is, there were two traveling salesmen that come in

speaker: HS
words: to Kendrick every week from Lewiston. There was two wholesale houses

speaker: HS
words: down there and these traveling men, one week they would hire an automobile

speaker: HS
words: and it would take em up to Sotithwick, Leland, Cameron,Cavendish, Tikon.

speaker: HS
words: They called it around the horn and back again. They'd both travel

speaker: HS
words: together and split the expense. The other week they would come up on

speaker: HS
words: the train and they'd just call up there, different ones. Of course,

speaker: HS
words: there was just one line to Southwick. Well, this one fellow he would

speaker: HS
words: go to the hotel and get the hotel man to get the different places on

speaker: HS
words: the line, and then he'd switch to him. While the other fellow would

speaker: HS
words: go to the telephone office and after he was through, he'd give em a

speaker: HS
words: fifty cent tip and pay his five cents. And so naturally when these two

speaker: HS
words: fellows come up, each one took out from that train just as fast as he

speaker: HS
words: could go, one for the hotel...but the operators knew that this other

speaker: HS
words: one was coming, the one at the hotel was first alright but they told

speaker: HS
words: him the line was busy, (laugh)

speaker: HS
words: And,this other fellow got in first all

speaker: HS
words: the time, (laugh laugh) They got their four bits, and the other fellow

speaker: HS
words: thought he was getting off cheap up there by saving his four bits, but

speaker: HS
words: he was second all the way around, (laugh laugh) So it seems that it

speaker: HS
words: was a hard proposition in those days, but nowadays pretty near everybody

speaker: HS
words: has their phones. But it used to be hard on the ones that have them.

speaker: HS
words: The neighbors would get em out. I remember people here that would call

speaker: HS
words: from the country, and call a person that had a phone at five o'clock in

speaker: HS
words: the morning and ask them if they wouldn't go to get somebody else to the

speaker: HS
words: phone. They're friends of theirs and they'd have to get up and dress to

speaker: HS
words: go do that, that time in the morning. Well it don't take long til people

speaker: HS
words: get tired of that and they say, well unless they do different in there

speaker: HS
words: we want our phone taken out. (cough) Did I say where I went to California

speaker: HS
words: did I?

speaker: ROB
words: Yes you did. How did it work around here when the...

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: How did things work around here when you had the switchboard type

speaker: ROB
words: operator that had to connect? Were there ever any problems with that?

speaker: HS
words: There was just the plugs to plug in, and the operator just had to listen

speaker: HS
words: to see who it was.

speaker: ROB
words: Could the operator always understand what they meant?

speaker: HS
words: She generally knew. The operator when she was on, pretty soon knew

speaker: HS
words: practically all by their voice. As I have in this article here, you know,

speaker: HS
words: this one women that called and she wanted Newt Heath. This operator, she

speaker: HS
words: should have given the number but the operator she knew it, and they'd

speaker: HS
words: usually ring em for em, so she rang a dentist because she thought if she

speaker: HS
words: needed new teeth why she oughta see the dentist. Same way, this fellow

speaker: HS
words: Mr. Heath he still lives over there.

speaker: ROB
words: His name is Newt Heath?

speaker: HS
words: He's pretty well up in years, but a vary nice family of them over there,

speaker: HS
words: and they have telephone service, and have had ever since then. They still

speaker: HS
words: do, I guess. I'd say the oldest one we have on, because he was on when

speaker: HS
words: Porter still had it already. But he's an oldtimers...

words: Did things like that happen very often? Like new teeth?

speaker: HS
words: No, there was only one that happened. This other one was about Annie

words: Wien. The Lewiston operator called and she (said) the lady down there

words: keeps calling in and asks why she can't get any wine in Kendrick. And

words: she says "Actually I think she's had too much already." (laugh) But

speaker: HS
words: she asked the operator, she says "I'm gonna let you talk to her." So

words: she put her on with the operator. Well, this operator recognized who

words: she was right away. Her daughter's name was Annie Wien, and so she rang

speaker: HS
words: her for her. (laugh) But any wine, Annie Wien, in Kendrick. She was

speaker: HS
words: a German women up here, a dandy bunch of people there. I don't even

speaker: HS
words: know whether she drank any wine or not.

speaker: ROB
words: But the operator thought she'd had too much already?

speaker: HS
words: Yes but the operator thought being she wanted some more wine and kept

speaker: HS
words: insisting on it, she thought that something was wrong someplace, (laugh)

speaker: ROB
words: Well when when you were stringing the line out to Bovill did many

speaker: ROB
words: unusual things or odd things happen? Was it difficult job or did tbings

speaker: ROB
words: go pretty smoothly?

speaker: HS
words: Well there was a few things that happened. It was a very wet year and

speaker: HS
words: it rained a lot. The soil up between Troy and Deary up in there was hard

speaker: HS
words: clay and the holes that were dug was half full of water. And a band of

speaker: HS
words: sheep came through one time and when we went to work Monday morning we

speaker: HS
words: must have pulled a half a dozen sheep out of the holes. In some holes

speaker: HS
words: there were two sheep. Most of them was alive and we give them to the

speaker: HS
words: farmer boys up there and they were tickled. We didn't know whose they

speaker: HS
words: were or anything. We told them if anyone came back and called for them

speaker: HS
words: they'd have to give them up, but they never give them up. I remember

speaker: HS
words: that there was a boy in Deary, he was around all the time we was working.

speaker: HS
words: I offered him fifty cents a day if he'd dip the water out of those holes

speaker: HS
words: as we went. I tied a can on the end of an anchor rod, and he'd have to

speaker: HS
words: dip down. And he dipped the holes out and he felt like he was doing al

speaker: HS
words: right, too.

speaker: ROB
words: How deep were the holes?

speaker: HS
words: About four, four and half feet. We had a negro that lives up there in

speaker: HS
words: our gang. He was just one the regular bunch. They all knew him, and I

speaker: HS
words: guess they went to sbhool together. They lived at Deary and he was just

speaker: HS
words: one of the bunch there. But he was kind of comical. I know that some

speaker: HS
words: time we'd lift or push pretty heavy on a pole and he'd say, "boy" he says

speaker: HS
words: "I got black in my face when I lifted on that thing" (laugh laugh) and

speaker: HS
words: sometime when we'd wash "it ain't no use me washing." But he was was

speaker: HS
words: kind of aclown. Dandy fellow and a good worker. There was one afternoon,

speaker: HS
words: one day I let a bunch of the men go ahead, all but Tony and I. We'd

speaker: HS
words: climb poles and tie the wires in on the insulators, and I sent the other

speaker: HS
words: crew into Bovill to do some work in there. They took off with this truck

speaker: HS
words: and went up to Clarkia where they could get some booze, and they got

speaker: HS
words: pretty well lit up. When they come back they didn't have the Negro on

speaker: HS
words: anymore, so they had to drive back and find him. They lost him, they

speaker: HS
words: lost him on the way. Iremember when they got back they had a deputy

speaker: HS
words: sheriff up there, and they had a showhouse up there. He said they was

speaker: HS
words: up there and he says "Gosh we got to have electricity in here" but he

speaker: HS
words: says "if they ever do that again" he says "I'll have to put em up."

speaker: HS
words: (laugh) But Ididn't let em have the truck anymore so that didn't happen

speaker: HS
words: anymore.

speaker: ROB
words: Well couldn't they get anything to drink in Bovill?

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: Why did they go all the way to Clarkia? Why couldn't they get any

speaker: ROB
words: thing in Bovill?

speaker: HS
words: There was nothing, it was dry then. Clarkia was open, and from here

speaker: HS
words: they used to go to Uniontown at times or down to Asotin, Washington.

speaker: ROB
words: Was it a thing each each town could vote on?

speaker: HS
words: I don't know just exactly how that was but it must have been, because

speaker: HS
words: they was both lumbering towns. But ah someway they went to Bovill to

speaker: HS
words: get this stuff...

speaker: ROB
words: They went to Clarkia?

speaker: HS
words: Clarkia yeh, that.was in 1927 so uh...

speaker: ROB
words: Well, did the work proceed pretty regularly? Did you get the the

speaker: ROB
words: line finished in the time you thought it would take you?

speaker: HS
words: We got the line into Deary, we got Deary in July. We got to Bovill

speaker: HS
words: within a few months, but then with five men of us we rebuilt the town

speaker: HS
words: of Bovill. We worked in there until the weather got bad, and we had it

speaker: HS
words: pretty well taken care of by that time. We was transferred back to bring

speaker: HS
words: the truck, Tony and I, down to Kendrick. It was all dirt roads, and

speaker: HS
words: snow on it and wet. We took off up there early in the morning, and I

speaker: HS
words: have some pictures of where we're prying on it to get it out of the mud

speaker: HS
words: holes, and we got down here by evening. But we had quite a time coming

speaker: HS
words: through there. I don't think there was a car, I believe we met a few

speaker: HS
words: fellows on horseback or something. I tell you wasn't nobody traveling.

speaker: HS
words: Oh, and another thing happened afterwards. It was about that time, just

speaker: HS
words: a little afterwards, I had charge of Juliaetta, Kendrick, Deary, and

speaker: HS
words: Bovill. They sent me a man down from Moscow out of the University, that

speaker: HS
words: they'd hired to help me do some work down here. I needed some work here

speaker: HS
words: and up at Deary and Bovill. So we went up to Bovill and Deary and we

speaker: HS
words: put in ground-rods. This man was George Brunzell. We drove em with a

speaker: HS
words: step ladder first and a sledge hammer, and then I went to a blacksmith

speaker: HS
words: here and I had a digger made like they use now to drive rods with, and

speaker: HS
words: he thought that was really the thing. And it was. Then here later years

speaker: HS
words: at a Trailblazer meeting, I still had this old driver, and I took it up

speaker: HS
words: to Spokane and presented it to Brunzell as the first instrument that he

speaker: HS
words: run for the Washington Water Power company.

speaker: ROB
words: He later became the president of the Washington Water Power?

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: He later became the president of the Washington Water Power?

speaker: HS
words: He was president here until just about a year ago. Often at gatherings

speaker: HS
words: (he would) point on me as his first employer, first man he worked for.

speaker: HS
words: He tells about driving these ground-rods. And he says the ground was

speaker: HS
words: hardpan up there, and sometimes we'd count and we'd get that rod down

speaker: HS
words: about an inch on thirty-six licks. We take turns-about. He tells all

speaker: HS
words: about how hard it was, and he says you couldn't tell what it would do.

speaker: HS
words: Ha said one time he was taking his rest while I was driving it, he was

speaker: HS
words: sitting on a bank, and he said the blame thing came right up under him.

speaker: HS
words: (laugh laugh) Course it wasn't that bad, but it made it a good story.

speaker: HS
words: He always enjoyed telling that.

speaker: ROB
words: Well how deep would you have to drive em?

speaker: HS
words: They were eight foot rods.

speaker: ROB
words: Drive them all the down?

speaker: HS
words: Yeh, umhuh.

speaker: ROB
words: (whistles)

speaker: HS
words: Yeh, and then this one I made, you see, was like they got now. Piece

speaker: HS
words: of pipe with a pretty heavy weight on top. Push that up and down, and

speaker: HS
words: the weight would be up above. You could stand on the ground. But hit

speaker: HS
words: ting it with the sledge hammer and then the fellow holding it, that was...

speaker: HS
words: well, it wasn't only that but neither one of us was too extra good on the sledge,

speaker: HS
words: you know. If you missed it and the other fellow's knuckles down below.

speaker: HS
words: He mentions in here how he hated to see that knuckle buster, well that's

speaker: HS
words: really what kept from getting his knuckles busted. It was just the other

speaker: HS
words: way, but then they got it switched around.

speaker: ROB
words: Were the people in Bovill pretty happy to see you when you came in

speaker: ROB
words: with power for em?

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: Were the people in Bovill pretty happy to see you when you came in

speaker: ROB
words: with power for em?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, to get their power. Yes, they was really tickled. They had a

speaker: HS
words: steam plant there that give era evening service. They had got it from

speaker: HS
words: a brick yard, as near as I understand, at Deary. There was gonna be

speaker: HS
words: a brick yard and it didn't materialize or something. But they had a

speaker: HS
words: fifty-five horsepower engine driving (I don't remember just what size

speaker: HS
words: generator it was) had a hundred fifty-five horsepower engine (that's

speaker: HS
words: right) driving I believe it was a fifty kilowat generator with a fiftyfive

speaker: HS
words: horsepower engine. No it with forty horsepower boiler. Furnished

speaker: HS
words: steam with a forty horsepower boiler, that's what they had there.

speaker: HS
words: Fellow by the name Hubert Hall was running it and he shut it down about

speaker: HS
words: midnight. So when we got the power in there we hooked up one transformer

speaker: HS
words: back in one place and we connected three street lights that were on his

speaker: HS
words: plant. We connected them on this transformer and after he turned his

speaker: HS
words: on, we watched and about the same time we turned these on. And when

speaker: HS
words: he closed down at night and went outside there, why all the lights weren't

speaker: HS
words: out. He thought that was quite a joke on him. He couldn't figure out

speaker: HS
words: what happened. He went back in and thought maybe something didn't stop

speaker: HS
words: or something, (laugh)

speaker: ROB
words: But he didn't know that you had...

speaker: HS
words: No, he didn't know it. He didn't know that we done that. We just

speaker: HS
words: done it just to see what he'd dri. (laugh laugh) Why then when we got

speaker: HS
words: to Kendrick here there was quite a few of us here. But in those days

speaker: HS
words: there was so many different things: the meter reading, and house wiring,

speaker: HS
words: and ah appliance repairing, selling, demonstrating, (company sold

speaker: HS
words: appliances,, ranges and stuff), and collecting and just whatever happened

speaker: HS
words: to be. Trouble-shooting. When anyone bought an electric range we

speaker: HS
words: would disconnect, stop the meter for ten days on am, so they could ex

speaker: HS
words: periment with it. Somebody go out and demonstrate and show em how to

speaker: HS
words: work it. Then at times our wives ware involved. Some woman would have

speaker: HS
words: to go out. Generally it was some switch that they didn't turn on or

speaker: HS
words: something they didn't do right. And ah...also had cooking school and

speaker: HS
words: our wives would usually go out and help with it. It was kind of a jackof-

speaker: HS
words: all-trades from line building, selling, and collecting, meter reading.

speaker: HS
words: But after it was here a few years they moved the office to Troy and I

speaker: HS
words: was left here as local representative. I took care of Juliaetta and

speaker: HS
words: Kendrick for a while, and then as the war came on they took out of the Troy office

speaker: HS
words: and Troy was served out of Moscow, and Deary and Bovill were transferred

speaker: HS
words: down to me again. I had those on top of these other two, with help out

speaker: HS
words: of Moscow if I needed it.

speaker: HS
words: Until M8, Elk River had been served by

speaker: HS
words: the lumber company since 1906. But when the saw mill lumber company

speaker: HS
words: quit, they also served the town. They built a line to Bovill and bought

speaker: HS
words: wholesale from the Washington Water Power Company. But in 1948 the

speaker: HS
words: Washington Water Power took it over so that added that on to this out of

speaker: HS
words: here. At the present time the man here takes care of Juliaetta, Kendrick,

speaker: HS
words: Deary, Bovill and Elk River. He takes a couple of trips a week up to Elk

speaker: HS
words: River, fifty miles and back, and just takes care of the moves, and trouble

speaker: HS
words: and whatever happens to be, and looks over the lines. It was pretty

speaker: HS
words: hard and tough sometimes, but then in those days fellows was younger and

speaker: HS
words: we enjoyed it. Being up there on snowshoes, sometimes all night hunting

speaker: HS
words: for trouble. Other people, some of them felt sorry for us being out

speaker: HS
words: all night like that and everything else and I just always think we do

speaker: HS
words: pretty good. I'd ask if they go hunting. They usually do. I says "don't

speaker: HS
words: you go up there in the same mountains?" Yes." I says 'do you always

speaker: HS
words: get what you're after? Do you climb around toot' "No, not always."

speaker: HS
words: But I says "look at the difference we got. We go up there and we know

speaker: HS
words: what we're going to find. We're going to find what we're after, and the

speaker: HS
words: longer it takes us to find it the more money we get." I said "now don't

speaker: HS
words: that beat hunting all to pieces." (laugh laugh) Going up there. Well

speaker: HS
words: sometimes be midnight, something happen up at Bovill. In the winter

speaker: HS
words: time they'd get too much load on a transformer and burn transformers

speaker: HS
words: out. I'd go up and check them out. Sometimes it was twenty or forty

speaker: HS
words: degrees below zero. If the transformer was burned out, call Pullman

speaker: HS
words: and have the crew bring out a new transformer. They'd come right out,

speaker: HS
words: and before morning they'd have service again. Most of them had oil

speaker: HS
words: burners in the house, but when it got cold it wasn't enough so they'd

speaker: HS
words: open the oven door and turn all the burners on, and it just put too much

speaker: HS
words: load on all the houses when they started to have ranges and they pour

speaker: HS
words: it on. But it's a whole lot different now that they got transformers

speaker: HS
words: with capacity enough. There isn't much worry about not taking it. But

speaker: HS
words: those days they had to watch an awful lot just when they put some load

speaker: HS
words: on as to whether it was going to hold it or not.

speaker: ROB
words: When you put the lines out to the farms on the ridges did the farmers

speaker: ROB
words: generally use very much electricity?

speaker: HS
words: No. (We installed them) first in 19 and 15 and 16. Actually Wilmot1s

speaker: HS
words: son-in-law came here and put in two diesel engines and served Troy and

speaker: HS
words: Kendrick both. And halfway between here and Troy they put on the Frank

speaker: HS
words: May place up there, and then they went the other way past and they put

speaker: HS
words: on I believe it was George Smith out of Troy. That was in 1916 and

speaker: HS
words: they didn't use enough so it paid to have a transformer kept agoin and

speaker: HS
words: everything else. So in 1925 Wilmot told me (I was kind of after him

speaker: HS
words: about building out to some people that had asked me about it on the

speaker: HS
words: ridges up here, about building lines, making some kind of a deal up

speaker: HS
words: here on American Ridge. They wanted some lights.) He said "I'll tell

speaker: HS
words: you what to do. If they buy the transformer and build a line, you go

speaker: HS
words: up there and help em. If you can get five of em up there to take it,

speaker: HS
words: I'll serve them and give em the same rate they got here in town." So

speaker: HS
words: the first fellow I went to was the furthest off. Yes he said, he'll

speaker: HS
words: buy a thousand dollars worth of equipment, motors and stuff. The next

speaker: HS
words: one I went to they says "well, we just rent here, how about wiring the

speaker: HS
words: house." He says "how about wiring the house. We just rent here from

speaker: HS
words: year to year and we wouldn't hardly feel like wiring the house, but can

speaker: HS
words: you get the landlord to wire the house." I asked the landlord. He

speaker: HS
words: said "I wouldn't be interested because I wouldn't get anymore rent" and

speaker: HS
words: so that was out. The next fellow, well he had a carbide plant and it

speaker: HS
words: was still working. And it dwindled down to three, and that is Walter

speaker: HS
words: Bingham (he had his own light plant first) and Ira Haven and ah Frank

speaker: HS
words: Benscoter, they didn't have anything. We wired the houses, all except

speaker: HS
words: Bingham's, and hooked them on up there. The following year (I still

speaker: HS
words: have the records, the ledgers, I looked it up here) there's one of

speaker: HS
words: them that hit 20 kilowatts and the other one didn't use ten, and the

speaker: HS
words: other one went about 15...

speaker: ROB
words: That's in a month?

speaker: HS
words: A month. Now that is a minimum of a dollar and a half...

speaker: ROB
words: Oh, for fifteen?

speaker: HS
words: No, ten was a minimum of a dollar and a half. (Fifteen) brought it up

speaker: HS
words: to about two and a quarter. But they was below that. There's one of

speaker: HS
words: the places that we had put in seventy-five watt lamps, and the lady was

speaker: HS
words: tickled. When her husband came home he took them to town and traded them

speaker: HS
words: all off for I believe it was twenty-fives, (laugh laugh) That kept theirs

speaker: HS
words: down. It just wasn't a paying proposition in '25, but things started to

speaker: HS
words: come then...

speaker: HS
words: (End of Side C)

speaker: ROB
words: What were the different jobs that the men did on the thrashing crew?

speaker: HS
words: Well, when we first started out, they used to come down off the ridge.

speaker: HS
words: Martin Thomas had a thrashing machine, a horse power machine. From this

speaker: HS
words: horse power equipment, the tumbling rod went to the thrashing machine

speaker: HS
words: and run the machine. My brother in those days was young and he was

speaker: HS
words: always around the machine and working. And a few years later he got a

speaker: HS
words: job going out and help oil on the thrashing machine.

speaker: ROB
words: What did the oiler do?

speaker: HS
words: He'd oil the whole machine. That is, see if any bearings got hot and

speaker: HS
words: at noon when it stood still oil all the bearings, and just keep it in

speaker: HS
words: shape. But he was also real interested in just the running of it and

speaker: HS
words: seemed to catch on awful easy about it. And so this fellow, the machine

speaker: HS
words: operator that run it, he'd see that Otto got on as oiler. So it helped

speaker: HS
words: him out too. So he started that way. Then later on I'd come in too

speaker: HS
words: and went out and pitched bundles. Those days we got two dollars and

speaker: HS
words: twenty-five cents an hour and started...

speaker: ROB
words: An hour or a day?

speaker: HS
words: A day, yes that's right. And get up early in the morning and get water

speaker: HS
words: out of the barrel and wash and have breakfast, and usually started out

speaker: HS
words: at 6 o'clock and maybe even before. Then in mid forenoon they'd come

speaker: HS
words: out with a lunch, they'd stop about 5 or 10 minutes. Then at noon hour

speaker: HS
words: had another lunch. And thrash til it kinda gets dark, then go in and

speaker: HS
words: have feed and go to bed. Except I remember one time that I was out

speaker: HS
words: hauling bundle, and a straw stack caught afire. And they moved the

speaker: HS
words: machine over kinda on a sloping slope, they moved it over on the other

speaker: HS
words: side of the valley and set up. It didn't take em long and it was going

speaker: HS
words: again. They had to let the fire burn down on the straw stack, they had

speaker: HS
words: it surrounded. Well, it lighted up the machine so I think we thrash til

speaker: HS
words: 9 or 10 o'clock that night cause we had light, (laugh) Later on, why

speaker: HS
words: I would usually go out and help on the separator :or something like that.

speaker: HS
words: And while I went out to pitch bundles, and sometimes I had a team out

speaker: HS
words: and haul bundles. Another time I bucked straw and I was hauling the straw

speaker: HS
words: to the engine cause it burnt straw. And then also when I'd catch up with

speaker: HS
words: the straw I'd usually help the fireman, relieve him. Cause we'd have to

speaker: HS
words: punch straw in with a fork, just keep pushing it in as fast as it would

speaker: HS
words: burn. If the steam went down you just pushed it in a little faster and

speaker: HS
words: if the steam got up why you'd slow down a little. And that's about the

speaker: HS
words: way it went.

speaker: ROB
words: Did they start the fire with straw too?

speaker: HS
words: They what?

speaker: ROB
words: Did they start the fire with straw too?

speaker: HS
words: Yeh, they just started the fire right out. That was straw it was and

speaker: HS
words: later some of them burned coal, but most of them burned straw those

speaker: HS
words: days. But they'd burn coal or wood when they was moving from one place

speaker: HS
words: to another. And then they'd usually burn coal or wood.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, the strawbuck would help the fire man out?

speaker: HS
words: The strawbuck, wasn't really his job but he'd generally do cause the

speaker: HS
words: fireman was held pretty steady there just to keep that going. A strawbuck

speaker: HS
words: if it wasn't pulling too heavy, or had good burning straw or some

speaker: HS
words: thing, why he didn't need as much straw. And he had a little time inbetween.

speaker: HS
words: He usually sometime help the fireman out, and let the fireman

speaker: HS
words: go get him a drink or one thing or another, and even for the fun of it.

speaker: HS
words: Just seemed like it felt right to do. The first machine I remember, the

speaker: HS
words: horsepower machine they had, it didn't blow the straw out like they do

speaker: HS
words: now. There was a draper...

speaker: ROB
words: This is combine you're talking about?

speaker: HS
words: No, a thrashing machine.

speaker: ROB
words: A horsepower thrashing machine?

speaker: HS
words: A thrashing machine. The horses went round and round and this machine

speaker: HS
words: had a draper which carried the straw out a ways, and they had one or two

speaker: HS
words: men on the straw pile to make a strawstack. Also to feed these bundles,

speaker: HS
words: they laid there and I think there's one man on each side with a knife,

speaker: HS
words: and he cut the bands on the bundles and stuck em in head first and spread

speaker: HS
words: em out. Stuck em in carefully so they went through even, because it was

speaker: HS
words: kinda smaller setup those days. Then they come out later with a knife

speaker: HS
words: that cut the bands and they just throwed em down in there. And later

speaker: HS
words: they come out with a blower on it and blew the straw out, after they

speaker: HS
words: got to using steam engines on em. And most of em got to be steam engines

speaker: HS
words: and later some of em went to gasoline. I think some of em burned diesel

speaker: HS
words: but I'm not too sure. They didn't call it diesel, they called it distilate

speaker: HS
words: or something a little different. I guess at that...

speaker: ROB
words: Well after the grain went through the thrashing machine, what happened

speaker: ROB
words: to it then?

speaker: HS
words: Well, there was a spout that come down first, and hook a sack on it.

speaker: HS
words: It switch then to two spouts, and they can switch from one sack holder

speaker: HS
words: to another. There'd be a sack put on the spout and that would be filling

speaker: HS
words: while they put a new sack on the other spout. Towards the last they

speaker: HS
words: had the bigger machines and they thrashed faster, they also had what they

speaker: HS
words: call two sack sewers and a jig. The sack sewers usaully sew up two sacks

speaker: HS
