words: and put em down and go back a little ways, and that's what they'd sit on.

speaker: HS
words: Then the jig would put a sack, to start out with, he would put a sack on

speaker: HS
words: each one of the fillers and he'd let one of them fill, and just as soon

speaker: HS
words: as that one got full enough he'd jig it down and carry it over it to one

speaker: HS
words: of the sack sewers.

speaker: ROB
words: What was jigging it down?

speaker: HS
words: Just to settle it down and get it about the right full. So if he spilled

speaker: HS
words: any, if he had too much in there he'd scoop it out on the ground under him,

speaker: HS
words: when they'd clean up it would be there where he was. Then he'd carry that

speaker: HS
words: sack 5 or 10 feet to wherever the sack sewers were. And they'd sit there

speaker: HS
words: and he'd set it between their knees and they'd kinda of get their knees

speaker: HS
words: around it and they'd sew it up. And then they'd bounce it up on the

speaker: HS
words: knees and get up and put it on the sack pile. Carry it off 10, or 15,

speaker: HS
words: 20 feet to wherever they started the sack pile. And this jig, when one

speaker: HS
words: sack would get got full he'd have an empty sack on the other place where

speaker: HS
words: it come out. He'd switch so it would put the grain into that sack.

speaker: HS
words: While he'd jig this one down and carry it over to the sack sewers, then

speaker: HS
words: put another new sack on it. By that time the other sack was about full.

speaker: HS
words: He'd jig that down, carry it over to the other sack sewer, and put

speaker: HS
words: another empty sack on it. And that went back and forth.

speaker: ROB
words: He never got much of a pause then either.

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: He never got much of a rest either.

speaker: HS
words: No, they never got any rest unless something broke down or something.

speaker: HS
words: Sometimes something like that would help out a little but...

speaker: ROB
words: Was the oiler always busy too? Was he kept busy just oiling?

speaker: HS
words: It seemed like the oiler and the separator tender, they was just mostly

speaker: HS
words: always watching and see if something was needed. Sometime they'd have

speaker: HS
words: a bearing that'd start to heat or something, he'd oil that occasionally

speaker: HS
words: oftener. Just kinda look over the thing. And usually in the noon hour

speaker: HS
words: they'd go through it and oil it completely again, and if any thing needed

speaker: HS
words: adjusting or something like that they'd adjust it up again and try to get

speaker: HS
words: it going in the afternoon again. Then they'd have lunch in the fore noon.

speaker: HS
words: But first they used to,there at our place when we had the thrashers

speaker: HS
words: there when I was smaller, my mother always had to cook for em and boarded

speaker: HS
words: em. They usually went home in the evening. There's quite a crew. And

speaker: HS
words: those days if a person really thinks about it, a hot stove in the kitchen

speaker: HS
words: you know, and then in the dining room all the tables around and people

speaker: HS
words: eating there, you could imagine how warm it would be there. It was

speaker: HS
words: really warm. They had an hour noon and they'd try to eat as fast as

speaker: HS
words: they could and get out and cool off enough, and then get out and go to

speaker: HS
words: work again. But later they got the cookhouses up on the ridges, and

speaker: HS
words: there was always two cooks. They'd have their lunch and meals there at

speaker: HS
words: the cookhouse, they had what they called a flunky. He was a roustabout

speaker: HS
words: and he'd get the food and get whatever they needed, water and things like

speaker: HS
words: that. Then they also had a waterbuck that had a tank, and he'd haul

speaker: HS
words: water for the engines and keep it agoing. It took quite a few people to

speaker: HS
words: run an outfit after they had one the size of the equipment.

speaker: ROB
words: How long would it take them to build up steam again in the morning

speaker: ROB
words: before they could get started?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, it's kinda aguess. Idon't know, I've never started a thrashing

speaker: HS
words: machine. But we'd run a tramway and had something practically similar

speaker: HS
words: to it. We could get up steam in twenty twenty-five minutes with kindlin

speaker: HS
words: and wood and fuel, but I wouldn't exactly know what it did take in the

speaker: HS
words: thrashing machine. I would say half hour or forty-five minutes, but

speaker: HS
words: that would be more or less of a guess.

speaker: ROB
words: Did the fireman get out earlier than everybody else and get working?

speaker: HS
words: I guess. Well, some of them had a watchman at night and I think he

speaker: HS
words: started the fire. Now I might be wrong on that, but I kinda believe

speaker: HS
words: that they did. They'd have somebody that looked around and done different

speaker: HS
words: things tthat had to be done through the night so they could get started

speaker: HS
words: in the morning.

speaker: ROB
words: Was he watching for fires or what?

speaker: HS
words: I'm not sure but whether they used some wood or coal and put in there

speaker: HS
words: to hold the fire over night, or whether they started it new in the

speaker: HS
words: morning. But it seemed like they was always ready to go when we was

speaker: HS
words: pitching bundles. We never got a chance to...(laugh) It was always

speaker: HS
words: ready to go.

speaker: ROB
words: Until when were the thrashing machines coming down here?

speaker: HS
words: What was that?

speaker: ROB
words: Until about what year were you working with thrashing machines instead

speaker: ROB
words: of combines?

speaker: HS
words: Well I remember we got a picture on our home place where Martin Thomas

speaker: HS
words: come down. And this picture was taken I believe it was in 1895. And that

speaker: HS
words: was a horse power rig. It used to be on American Ridge. I believe that was

speaker: HS
words: the only one on the end of the ridge that used to come down there. Then

speaker: HS
words: about 1910 Ibelieve it was, Ithink Otto started to go up. 08, 1907 or

speaker: HS
words: 08 that was, oiling. And 1910 or 11 Iwould either go out and pitch

speaker: HS
words: bundles or go out and haul bundles. Bucked straw for a number of years and

speaker: HS
words: then when that wound up, maybe even before, we'd run the tramway. Just as

speaker: HS
words: soon as they started to haul towards the warehouse, we'd go and take care

speaker: HS
words: of the warehouse and run the tramway. And that's when we kinda quit the

speaker: HS
words: thrashing there and went to the tramming business. We handled it after

speaker: HS
words: they had it in the sacks.

speaker: ROB
words: Were there any serious accidents with that tramway? Did it ever get

speaker: ROB
words: away?

speaker: HS
words: No, it got away before my times. Mother said that it got away, someway.

speaker: HS
words: The upper end of it it went up the hill and it came in more on a flat. It

speaker: HS
words: seems as though when they stopped it the car someway...1've heard different

speaker: HS
words: stories. I've heard that they had the lower one unhooked and they couldn't

speaker: HS
words: hold it, and I've heard that they backed it up someway and that car put

speaker: HS
words: slack in the cable and unhooked and took off. But mother said that she was

speaker: HS
words: hanging out clothes and she heard a noise and all she could see on the sidehill

speaker: HS
words: over there was a bunch of dust. And it seemed like this car went on

speaker: HS
words: down and there was...

speaker: ROB
words: Was it a empty car or a full car?

speaker: HS
words: This was full. It was steep and at the bottom of this steep pitch there

speaker: HS
words: was a bench that went out a little ways. And it seems as though when it

speaker: HS
words: hit that bench, they tell me that it took right straight out and left the

speaker: HS
words: track. Went out in space and just scattered everything there was down over

speaker: HS
words: the hillside there. And that part now whether...my idea is more or less

speaker: HS
words: it just got away from them. I don't know why they unhooked it or just didn't

speaker: HS
words: have any safety hooks on it or something, and it got away anyway. Or else

speaker: HS
words: if it was that they didn't fasten the lower one, why then of course just

speaker: HS
words: the cable itself would come up instead of the car on it. But they tell me

speaker: HS
words: when they first started out they used a block and tackle, and I believe a

speaker: HS
words: horse, and pulled the top car out off this level place until it got to the

speaker: HS
words: front of the hill, and the loaded car would bring an empty one up. But that

speaker: HS
words: didn't prove out very good. Then they put in this steam engine, it had a

speaker: HS
words: twin cylinder engine with two drums. The cable would come up and keep go

speaker: HS
words: ing back and forth. They'd go back under and go around the back drum, and

speaker: HS
words: then go around the front one, back to the back one, around the front one,

speaker: HS
words: and back and forth about a dozen times or so until it went back out again.

speaker: HS
words: Then they had band brakes on it, and the engine could be turned on or off.

speaker: HS
words: Whenever the load got out to the front of the hill they could uncouple the

speaker: HS
words: engine and just hold it with the brake and let it coast down. And they'd

speaker: HS
words: jump the track once in awhile, but otherwise in my time...there was one

speaker: HS
words: time before, a car come down and bump something out and some timbers fell

speaker: HS
words: on somebody and hurt him I remember, one time.

speaker: HS
words: But otherwise the cars was

speaker: HS
words: fixed so they wouldn't hardly get off the cable, and if they did get into

speaker: HS
words: the brush at the top what we usually had to do is fire up and get a little

speaker: HS
words: more steam. But they had engine enough there to pull a car right back out

speaker: HS
words: of the brush, back up again. And they'd pull it up aways and work it back

speaker: HS
words: on the rails and sometimes they'd even have to unload it to get it on.

speaker: HS
words: Then put it on there. It was quite a tram there. After the trucks come in,

speaker: HS
words: why then the people quit hauling to the tramway and more hauled down over

speaker: HS
words: the hill. It wasn't taken care of, and I think it was 1917 or 18 it was

speaker: HS
words: taken and sold for scrap. It was taken down. A funny incident happened

speaker: HS
words: there. I wasn't here at that time, but they took the rails all loose,

speaker: HS
words: pulled the spikes out. It was iron rails, just like the railroad rails

speaker: HS
words: but a small size. They some way had a block, and tackle on the lower end,

speaker: HS
words: and they was going to pull it down one length at a time. They couldn't

speaker: HS
words: get it to go someway or something, I don't know whether they didn't have

speaker: HS
words: the tail end up there tied, but it broke loose. When it did get started,

speaker: HS
words: the rail just started on coming and just doubled around on the side hill

speaker: HS
words: over there. One fellow that was up on the sidehill helping along to get

speaker: HS
words: it started, he said he took behind a tree. He said the rails would come

speaker: HS
words: on both sides on there and they'd double up around the tree and everything

speaker: HS
words: else. Brother's got a picture of a rod...right at the time being there's

speaker: HS
words: a rail sticking in a tree up there, that the end of the rail went into that

speaker: HS
words: tree I don't know how far. It had taken apart where the coupling was and

speaker: HS
words: there was about 2 or 3 feet sticking out of that tree now, in that tree

speaker: HS
words: where that went in there. We used to like to run that tramway, it was

speaker: HS
words: quite a job. Nowadays they work it entirely different. We lived just

speaker: HS
words: across from the warehouse. Take us about ten minutes on the bicycle to

speaker: HS
words: get over to the lower warehouse, and walk up the tram and get steam up by

speaker: HS
words: seven o'clock.

speaker: HS
words: We'd usually leave the house at six o'clock and get over

speaker: HS
words: to the warehouse at ten minutes after six and walk up over the hill to

speaker: HS
words: the top in about twenty minutes. We'd be up there at half past. Start a

speaker: HS
words: fire in the boiler and load the first car out. And then whatever help

speaker: HS
words: you had, either one or two men, they'd come up on the first tram then. But

speaker: HS
words: the first tram always run just practically at seven o'clock. And then at

speaker: HS
words: night the help rode down on the last tram. And then the fellow had to

speaker: HS
words: walk down to get down. Those days why we didn't have automobles; the last

speaker: HS
words: year we run we had motorcycles, but it didn't even dawn on us to ride a

speaker: HS
words: motorcycle around. We just took up the track and made our money. We

speaker: HS
words: made thirty cents an hour, three dollars a day. That was good meney those

speaker: HS
words: days. But on going down there was days that we didn't run the tram, but

speaker: HS
words: just went up to the upper warehouse to receive grain when the farmers

speaker: HS
words: brought it in. So it dawned on me that I could make a rig and ride down.

speaker: HS
words: I took some 4 x 4's, tore them off the warehouse up there in different

speaker: HS
words: places, and put them on the rail lengthwise about three or four feet long.

speaker: HS
words: Nailed a 1 x 4 or something on the inside for a flange, nailed a board

speaker: HS
words: across the back for a seat, and nailed two 2 x 6»s across the front with a

speaker: HS
words: space inbetween em so it made a sled that fit on the track. Couldn't jump

speaker: HS
words: off. Then take it out to the front of the hill and bent a piece of rod like

speaker: HS
words: a letter S and hook it between the space between the two front timbers.

speaker: HS
words: Put a pipe and hook it around the cable, around underneath there was always

speaker: HS
words: a cable laid between one track or the other, and then use the pipe on top

speaker: HS
words: and lifted this cable up underneath. I'd use that for a brake. And so

speaker: HS
words: that worked fine. So everytime there was any slack and I didn't have one

speaker: HS
words: of those jiggers up there, everytime we had to go up and receive grain, we'd

speaker: HS
words: make another one. By the time we got through we had five or six of em.

speaker: HS
words: And then whenever the first tram run we loaded them all up and haul them

speaker: HS
words: all back again. And there was quite an incident that happened there one

speaker: HS
words: time. E. W. Porter owned this warehouse (also the banker). There was a

speaker: HS
words: circus coming to town in the afternoon and school was out I quess that day,

speaker: HS
words: and Porter told us (it seemed like all the farmers was coming down to the

speaker: HS
words: circus) that we didn't have to tram that afternoon if we trammed in the

speaker: HS
words: forenoon. Why if it was alright with the farmers up there, if they wasn't

speaker: HS
words: gonna haul, we'll talk to over with them, why we could go to the circus.

speaker: HS
words: So this boy Marion Porter, he was a young fellow, he wanted to ride down

speaker: HS
words: with me. He says "I'd like a ride down with you when you go down on one."

speaker: HS
words: We called em go-devils. So he stayed up there when we run the last tram.

speaker: HS
words: We took it out to the front of the hill and we got on it. The first place

speaker: HS
words: is steep. It starts out steep and there's this bench down below, and as we

speaker: HS
words: started down this steep place I tried to slow it down and this rod we had

speaker: HS
words: bent in the letter S started to straighten out. And so I told him I says

speaker: HS
words: "now it don't make any difference how fast we go when we hit that bench

speaker: HS
words: we're going to have to get off, but I'll slow it down the best I can."

speaker: HS
words: And so I told him "when I say go why you get off on your side, you get

speaker: HS
words: ready, and I'll get off over here." And so we got down there and I got

speaker: HS
words: it slowed down pretty well but still going. "Get off" and he got off on

speaker: HS
words: one side and I got off on the other. We bumped a little bit to ourselves

speaker: HS
words: but not hurt anything. But I could still hear him when it went down there

speaker: HS
words: and the dust was flying, he said "gee aren't you glad we aren't on it now."

speaker: HS
words: And it went right down there and knocked onto the bumper and knocked the

speaker: HS
words: bumper right into the lower car. Just hit the end of it and it smashed

speaker: HS
words: itself all to pieces down there. We had to walk down the rest of the way.

speaker: HS
words: After that whenever we rode double we always took two of em. And tied em

speaker: HS
words: together and instead of using a hook we put wire around it. Wrap a bunch

speaker: HS
words: of wire. But that would always sing. You could hear it all over the valley

speaker: HS
words: when you come down, you could hear that thing sing. Just singing away, the

speaker: HS
words: wire sliding ever the cable. We had a lot of fun out of it too.

speaker: ROB
words: Wasn't there a series of pretty bad train wrecks around here?

speaker: HS
words: On, train wrecks. Yes there was a train wreck when they was building I

speaker: HS
words: believe it was a line that runs up the Clearwater, to Stites. I believe

speaker: HS
words: that was the one. They was hauling rails down for it. They was up at

speaker: HS
words: Troy and I've heard different stories. They say that they didn't want to

speaker: HS
words: take a chance of going down, it was frosty and one thing or another. But

speaker: HS
words: any way they was ordered to go down with the rails and they came down and

speaker: HS
words: couldn't hold it. And as they got down towards up around Kendrick up here,

speaker: HS
words: the conductor cut the caboose loose, and held and saved himself and another

speaker: HS
words: fellow. The rest of it kept on going. They went and derailed right up into

speaker: HS
words: the upper end of Kendrick here, and went into Potlatch Creek. I think there

speaker: HS
words: was two or three of them, engineers both of the engineers and fireman I

speaker: HS
words: believe it was (I've got more accurate data of it someplace) was killed.

speaker: HS
words: Then they had one or two hearings, trials. This fellow cut himself loose

speaker: HS
words: with the caboose, and it seems as though he got canned because they figured

speaker: HS
words: if he would have kept that caboose on it would help hold the tail end back,

speaker: HS
words: and it would have alright too. But it was quite a mess. I can just

speaker: HS
words: remember going up and see the engines laying in there. It's just been a

speaker: HS
words: few years ago there's just still a rail...they were working in that creek

speaker: HS
words: and there's just still a rail down in there which was down in there. I've

speaker: HS
words: got a picture of that. I've also got the story of this wreck, also of

speaker: HS
words: the trial.

speaker: ROB
words: Didn't Kendrick have quite a problem in the early days with fires, floods

speaker: ROB
words: and stuff like that?

speaker: HS
words: Well it seemed like it didn't have near as much trouble with fires as they

speaker: HS
words: had later on, cause it didn't have any equipment that set anything a fire

speaker: HS
words: much. It was only smoking was the only thing. And it seemed like those

speaker: HS
words: days that more smoked a pipe or something or cigar, and smoke it not at

speaker: HS
words: work time. But there wasn't as much that it did catch. It seemed like

speaker: HS
words: the only trouble they had would be astraw stack catch afire or something

speaker: HS
words: like that. But later on now, with these trucks and one thing and another,

speaker: HS
words: the exhaust Ithink that's where they catch it. It throws out some spark,

speaker: HS
words: or soot would accumulate and then a hot one would go out into the dry grass

speaker: HS
words: and set it afire. But now the hardest of it was pulling the machine from

speaker: HS
words: one place to another. On account of ditches and up hill and one thing and

speaker: HS
words: another they'd hook it up and pull it with the engines. But they had to

speaker: HS
words: cross...I remember they brought in a new machine one time in Juliaetta

speaker: HS
words: and they was going to thrash our home place down there first. That was

speaker: HS
words: about 1912 or so, I'd say 11 or 12, and unloaded it down there at Juliaetta.

speaker: HS
words: Then, they couldn't go across the bridges with it, they had to kinda make

speaker: HS
words: a road alongside and hold the front of the engine up so it was down, so

speaker: HS
words: it didn't rear up over the bank. It was quite a chore to cross ditches

speaker: HS
words: like that, get from one place to another. It was alright in the fields

speaker: HS
words: when they could go from one field to another, but if they had to go further

speaker: HS
words: it was kinda hard to get anyone down to where we were some years. Why you

speaker: HS
words: always tried to find out ahead of time whether we'd be able to get anyone

speaker: HS
words: to thrash, and if we didn't we'd just have to thrash the hay down in a

speaker: HS
words: place hard to get at. But it used to be about noon you'd hear the whistles

speaker: HS
words: blow,three or four of em around the thrashing machines...

speaker: HS
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: ROB
words: There were quite a few floods here in the early days weren't there?

speaker: HS
words: Yes it used to be that we'd earn a few dollars once in awhile when we got

speaker: HS
words: bigger, when they'd have some floods or washouts. Expecially on one in

speaker: HS
words: 1911 I remember down there they had a cloud burst that covered up the tracks,

speaker: HS
words: and they run the trains in on each side, the passenger trains. Transferred

speaker: HS
words: and carried everything from one side to the other. You used to get a job

speaker: HS
words: helping doing that. Then they had it shoveled out so the trains could get

speaker: HS
words: through and it seemed like high water would come up in the gulch and it

speaker: HS
words: would cover the tracks up every so often and we'd be called out to kelp

speaker: HS
words: shovel em out. And so we got pretty good pay. We didn't mind when they

speaker: HS
words: did get covered up. And that place for awhilethere seemed to bother. But

speaker: HS
words: in earlier years I could remember about 1900 or 1901, there was two wash

speaker: HS
words: outs there. One of the years there's a work train came down with a cook

speaker: HS
words: house and some sleeping cars which they just had fixed up, and the caboose

speaker: HS
words: and a car or two with the tools and hand cars. They was going down below

speaker: HS
words: Juliaetta. But as they got this side of Juliaetta there on middle Potlatch

speaker: HS
words: creek, that bridge had washed out so they couldn't go any further. So

speaker: HS
words: they was going to go back to Kendrick but by that time it had washed the

speaker: HS
words: track out just about a quarter of a mile behind em. And they couldn't

speaker: HS
words: get it back. So in the evening there was, I don't know how many of em, but

speaker: HS
words: there was a lady cook and her husband came up to our place. And there's

speaker: HS
words: some others- that needed sleep for the night, they came up and slept in

speaker: HS
words: the barn. And this other stayed down and watched to see what's going and

speaker: HS
words: run what they had left. First it took the caboose, then took the cars in

speaker: HS
words: one after the other during the night. They'd run that engine ahead and just

speaker: HS
words: keep it, and they saved it and I think one car or two. The rest of it went

speaker: HS
words: down and it took a railroad bridge out a little further. It went to the

speaker: HS
words: tramway and that gave em still more.

speaker: HS
words: And that went down and took the bridge

speaker: HS
words: out at Juliaetta there by the depot. On this set-up there was a caboose

speaker: HS
words: and a couple cars over across the creek, about where the tramway was just a

speaker: HS
words: little below where it went in. One or two had gone in and landed down

speaker: HS
words: below Juliaetta, hung up on a place down there. And then later they came

speaker: HS
words: down with equipment and pulled them over and put em back on the tracks

speaker: HS
words: again, got em out. We used to go over there and play in that caboose. It

speaker: HS
words: was sitting there kinda in a sand pile leaning towards one side. You went

speaker: HS
words: in kinda made you feel dizzy and kinda goofy. But we should have bought

speaker: HS
words: that caboose because I went places and they showed me where water would run

speaker: HS
words: up hill, and the ball would roll up hill. But that's what it did in that

speaker: HS
words: caboose. We used to go in there because it sure made you think...the optical

speaker: HS
words: illusion there made you think that the stuff rolled up hill. So we should

speaker: HS
words: have bought that caboose and put a sign there, and we would have had some

speaker: HS
words: thing. But anyway they took it out. I still remember the name of the people.

speaker: HS
words: Neff was the name of the cook and his wife. They seem to be nice people

speaker: HS
words: someway. I just remembered their name. And I believe it was the next year

speaker: HS
words: or either the year before or the year afterwards, one was 1900 I think and

speaker: HS
words: one 1901. Heimgartners came to Juliaetta to locate. They came on a train,

speaker: HS
words: but when they got to Troy the tracks had washed out to Kendrick and they

speaker: HS
words: couldn't get any further. It was muddy in the wintertime, spring time I

speaker: HS
words: think it was. So they hired two livery rigs, two. hacks, to bring the trunks

speaker: HS
words: and stuff. I think there was eight in their family at the time. And they

speaker: HS
words: brought them down on the muddy roads and they couldn't get to Juliaetta

speaker: HS
words: because the bridge had washed out on Middle Potlatch there. So we was the

speaker: HS
words: first place located on the other side that looked like it was able to put

speaker: HS
words: up a few of fern, so they stopped and come back that far. The folks made

speaker: HS
words: room for 'em some way, and unloaded their stuff there and the livery rigs

speaker: HS
words: went on back. They stayed a couple, three days til some way fixed so they

speaker: HS
words: could get across the creek down there. Made it out all right. Some of them

speaker: HS
words: slept in the barn.

speaker: HS
words: They often mentioned about it, how they got there and

speaker: HS
words: stayed there and remembered the place. So that's two (washouts). Then

speaker: HS
words: they had another washout, later years. I've got some pictures of washouts

speaker: HS
words: below Juliaetta, down about four miles where it took out track. It was all

speaker: HS
words: laying in the creek for about a couple hundred yards. And down below Julia

speaker: HS
words: etta where the water's over the rails. And up here at the depot where the

speaker: HS
words: water was where you couldn't see any ties, just the top of the rails stuck

speaker: HS
words: out. But it always would do damage someplace along. There wouldn't be

speaker: HS
words: any trains for a couple, three days. 1927 I had a wet snow up in the

speaker: HS
words: Bovill country, I believe it was Thanksgiving Day, and it rained into it

speaker: HS
words: and the wind came into it and it all came down through here. It washed the

speaker: HS
words: track out just below the Juliaetta Depot and a work train went down. The

speaker: HS
words: engine rolled in and one or two cars I think with it. And also year or two

speaker: HS
words: later, first of April I had a flood that went over the tracks. Washed out

speaker: HS
words: different places and Kendrick was isolated. They couldn't get either

speaker: HS
words: direction. They brought the mail up with a plane and dropped it up on a

speaker: HS
words: hillside here. So as far as washouts and train wrecks and something that's

speaker: HS
words: a kinda interesting place. Then of course there was a train wreck that they

speaker: HS
words: had. They was building the Stites railroad I believe it was, a Stites branch

speaker: HS
words: over there. They's coming down with quite a few cars of rails and iron and

speaker: HS
words: they had two engines on it. When they left Troy they didn't think they'd

speaker: HS
words: make it, but they told them to go on. But as they got down there they couldn't

speaker: HS
words: hold it. And the caboose fellow, the conductor, he cut the caboose loose and

speaker: HS
words: he got it stopped, but the rest of the train came down just to the edge of

speaker: HS
words: town up here. Both engines rolled in and I don't know how many people it

speaker: HS
words: killed. I think it was both engineers and both fireman. I could remember

speaker: HS
words: seeing it laying in there. I have a picture of it too, where they've taking

speaker: HS
words: it but afterwards. And also they had a trial on this fellow that cut this

speaker: HS
words: caboose loose. He wasn't suppose to do that. If he'd left it on they figured

speaker: HS
words: it might have held back enough. I believe the history is that they fired

speaker: HS
words: him. That was one of the big ones I can remember.

speaker: HS
words: One morning they called

speaker: HS
words: up R. H. Porter down here in Juliaetta and told him that-the engine had got

speaker: HS
words: away from 'em up at Troy and was heading down the tracks. And if it got

speaker: HS
words: that far they wanted him to go over there right away and put some ties on

speaker: HS
words: the tracks. If there's a ties a stack of ties down there across from where

speaker: HS
words: he lived then put 'em on someway so it would wreck that engine so it couldn't

speaker: HS
words: go any further. And he did that but it didn't get very far. It stopped up

speaker: HS
words: above here. It seemed like they dumped it on a sidetrack up there and it

speaker: HS
words: lost some of the wheels under the coal car or something, and it drug it

speaker: HS
words: back enough so it didn't get this far. Another time I remember we was coming

speaker: HS
words: between here and Juliaetta and a bunch of cars went by. I believe it was

speaker: HS
words: four cars loaded with cattle went by with a pretty good lick of speed, faster

speaker: HS
words: than a train usually goes. And pretty soon an engine come along awhile after

speaker: HS
words: wards behind it. But seems like they went pretty careful, they wasn't going

speaker: HS
words: too fast. And in about a couple of hours why this engine, come back bringing

speaker: HS
words: those cars of cattle back up again. They got down below Arrow where it got

speaker: HS
words: leveler and it run into the back of a freight train. This freight train was

speaker: HS
words: also traveling so it was lucky. We got a lot of things when we was kids

speaker: HS
words: to see like that those days. Wrecks. Here not too many years ago they had,

speaker: HS
words: I believe it was a snow plow that they'd tied on the back. The couplings

speaker: HS
words: were out or something. They tied it on the back of a freight train that

speaker: HS
words: went up out of Kendrick, and it got up above about a mile or little over a

speaker: HS
words: mile. Why, it broke loose and went on down the track. So they backed the

speaker: HS
words: freight train up in order to get or to catch it. Anyway the freight train

speaker: HS
words: then jumped the track on the corner and two or three cars of frozen food

speaker: HS
words: went down over the bank about a hundred feet. Of course immediately they

speaker: HS
words: got some refrigerated automobliles, trucks, to get the stuff out. Then

speaker: HS
words: later they put the cars back up on the tracks. There's usually fires along

speaker: HS
words: the tracks up there, used to be that the engines spark, brakes would spark

speaker: HS
words: and have trouble.

speaker: HS
words: No, the railroad had a quite a time with troubles between

speaker: HS
words: you might say between Arrow and just above Kendrick here. I've got some

speaker: HS
words: pictures where there's a track down here about four miles below Juliaetta.

speaker: HS
words: It must be, oh couple three hundred yards where the track is laying in the

speaker: HS
words: river. Underwashed it and it just settled down in. And down below Juliaetta

speaker: HS
words: where the water was over the railroad and over the highway, and its been

speaker: HS
words: quite often that we'd have no train service on account of high water. And

speaker: HS
words: the town here in 1901 or 02, 1901 I believe it was, that it washed across

speaker: HS
words: up here where that train went in someway. I've heard that it damaged a fill,

speaker: HS
words: some of it that held a creek back. Well anyway, the creek come through up

speaker: HS
words: there and came down through town and I know it left about a foot or two of

speaker: HS
words: mud in the store. I can just remember being up here one time with mother

speaker: HS
words: at McGrew's Store (it stood where the park now is) there was mud that they

speaker: HS
words: had to get outAthe store. And down here right at the first turn of the street

speaker: HS
words: down here, there was Hamlin or Hamley lived there, and Fred Crocker went in to

speaker: HS
words: rescue *em out. They was surrounded with water. (Went in) with a team and

speaker: HS
words: buggy and as he came out I guess something came down and hit the buggy and

speaker: HS
words: also it went into a hole underneath and two of the children spilled out and

speaker: HS
words: they drowned. So it wasn't just washouts. I don't know, did I mention about

speaker: HS
words: at Arrow they had this high water that washed out quite a stretch of track

speaker: HS
words: there and put it over on the other side of the creek. The whole thing, the

speaker: HS
words: bridge of Howard Gulch and all just undermined it all and just swung it over

speaker: HS
words: to the other side of the river. It was on a turn.

speaker: HS
words: They build a new track

speaker: HS
words: and filled in. Built a new track in there. We had some more high water

speaker: HS
words: afterwards but it wasn't really what they consider high water. But anyway

speaker: HS
words: it had underwashed some of this fill that they'd made right at that place,

speaker: HS
words: and there's a passenger train that went down at night about 10:30 or 11

speaker: HS
words: o'clock. They usually had a baggage car and a mail car and two or three

speaker: HS
words: passenger cars. I have a picture of that too there. But they went in, the

speaker: HS
words: engine rolled in and the first cars they didn't uncouple, but the last ones

speaker: HS
words: stayed on the tracks. Some of them was halfway down. The engine was down

speaker: HS
words: and they never did find a fireman, his name was Skidmore I remember. I

speaker: HS
words: don't remember whether the engineer got out or whether he was drowned. But

speaker: HS
words: it was a bad wreck, it didn't materialize. Well in those railroad days it

speaker: HS
words: was interesting to think about the transportation, when there'd be maybe a

speaker: HS
words: hundred Italians on the crew. They'd have these handcars, pumpcars. They'd

speaker: HS
words: have maybe a couple dozen of them tied together. They'd go up and down the

speaker: HS
words: track on 'em singing and having a good time, just a pumping away. Instead

speaker: HS
words: of running them single they had them all tied together, and everybody was

speaker: HS
words: a pumping and pumping those things going up and down the tracks. All the

speaker: HS
words: railroad work was done by pump cars like that. And take above town, they

speaker: HS
words: couldn't pump »em up so they'd have flat car, and when the morning passenger

speaker: HS
words: came up sometime between eight and nine o'clock they had a rope, and they'd

speaker: HS
words: put this rope around something on the back of the passenger train. When

speaker: HS
words: they got up to the furtherest end of the job why they'd cut themselve loose.

speaker: HS
words: And do their work. And then all they needed to do is get on and hold the

speaker: HS
words: brake and come down, and then they'd coast back home again in the evenings.

speaker: HS
words: But a little later on they made them stop doing that, and then they come

speaker: HS
words: out with the gasoline rigs and went that way. It used to take a train about

speaker: HS
words: a hour to go from Kendrick to Troy, eleven or twelve miles. I remember we

speaker: HS
words: were on a picnic about 1915, 1916 up above Kendrick a couple three miles

speaker: HS
words: and a passenger train came up. Had one or two cars of fruit, refrigerated

speaker: HS
words: fruit, on it. It would come in on a slow speed, and another boy and I would

speaker: HS
words: climb on these fruit cars, and we rode up the track quite aways. We saw a

speaker: HS
words: pushcar down there sitting on the side of the track. We got off a little

speaker: HS
words: further up and walked down to this pushcar. It had a hole knocked out that

speaker: HS
words: you could take a pole or something and put brake on one of the wheels with

speaker: HS
words: it. So we got it on the track and we rode down to where the picnic was on

speaker: HS
words: that pushcar. We used to play around on the railroad track quite a lot.

speaker: HS
words: When they were slow we'd go up to Clyde Spur (on our telephone work)

speaker: HS
words: up there about three miles above town, and we'd just get off. Most of the

speaker: HS
words: places we know about where the train would slow down quite a lot, and we'd

speaker: HS
words: always get off.

speaker: ROB
words: You say you used to ride the trains a lot as a kid?

speaker: HS
words: Yeh, we rode the train. The trains was full. It would be Fourth of July

speaker: HS
words: celebration, and specially between Juliaetta and Kendrick it just didn't

speaker: HS
words: have standing room hardly. It would be clear full. Ten cents one way or

speaker: HS
words: fifteen cents round trip. I've got some tickets dated 1912 yet. Someway

speaker: HS
words: they didn't use or take up. It used to be by train going to work, or train

speaker: HS
words: going to Lewiston °r Spokane or someplace. (You'd) go by train. We used

speaker: HS
words: to come up here and roller skate I can remember in early years. Two of us

speaker: HS
words: or three or four of us, we'd come up here and we'd always buy a return trip

speaker: HS
words: ticket. We'd get 'em cheaper and they was good for a month and we always

speaker: HS
words: had one or two in our pocket either way. But anyway we'd go out on the

speaker: HS
words: back platform in the winter and they wouldn't come out to take tickets there

speaker: HS
words: cause they didn't think anyone was out there, so we'd have that ticket for

speaker: HS
words: the next time. Well, one time I remember that there was a couple more fellows

speaker: HS
words: came with us and we all went out on the back platform. They went along too.

speaker: HS
words: And the conductor came out. These fellows thought they was gonna get the

speaker: HS
words: ride for nothing you know, and the conductor got out and the rest of us had

speaker: HS
words: tickets cause we always had one. But one fellow he didn't have a ticket, so

speaker: HS
words: the conductor took his hat and he told him the next morning if he'd come

speaker: HS
words: down there and bring his dime he'd give him the hat back. So we rustled his

speaker: HS
words: ten cents and ppid his way. I think there was two of 'em. Well, as youngsters

speaker: HS
words: you know they'll always figure out something. So in order to have a little

speaker: HS
words: fun there was about eight or ten of us got on that train. We passed the

speaker: HS
words: conductor and all but two of »em got out the back and jumped off and walked

speaker: HS
words: down to Juliaetta. They just wanted to see what would happen.

speaker: HS
words: These other

speaker: HS
words: two, my brother was one of 'em, wanted to see. Said they hunted all the way

speaker: HS
words: to Juliaetta, under the seats and everyplace else looking for where the rest

speaker: HS
words: of those guys were. But we was on foot going (laugh) to Juliaetta. We

speaker: HS
words: thought that was a pretty good joke. (Laugh.) Yep, I remember on telephone

speaker: HS
words: work we used to go on passenger trains down to Arrow, and then catch a pass

speaker: HS
words: enger train up to Myrtle, up to the other side, and then walk across the hill

speaker: HS
words: over there. You could go up on the other side and have a team meet you on

speaker: HS
words: the other side of the creek about four miles below Juliaetta and come back

speaker: HS
words: again. Other times we'd have troubles down toward Arrow. We'd go down there

speaker: HS
words: and play horseshoes down there (somebody had a horseshoe set down there)

speaker: HS
words: until the freight train came up and we'd get on that freight train. But

speaker: HS
words: sometime they wouldn't stop here. Usually when they stopped to take water

speaker: HS
words: we could get off. But we lived at Juliaetta and we'd have to walk from

speaker: HS
words: Juliaetta and back. But that beat walking all the way. I remember one time

speaker: HS
words: where they didn't stop, but as soon as they got on the hill they slowed

speaker: HS
words: down enough so we'd get off. We had a little further to walk, but still it

speaker: HS
words: wasn't as far as walking home from Arrow. But we used to ride that freight

speaker: HS
words: train back every once in awhile. Get on it. Sometimes they'd never see

speaker: HS
words: us, and if they did we'd tell 'em what we did. They usually just all good,

speaker: HS
words: even a section boss. He loaned us a speeder to use on the tracks. The

speaker: HS
words: little hand speeder, had rubber tires on it to go back and forth. There's

speaker: HS
words: a whole lot more..now they wouldn't dare to do anything like that because

speaker: HS
words: if something would happen why they'd sue them for the rest of what they was

speaker: HS
words: worth, you know. But those days everybody helped together more. There

speaker: HS
words: wasn't anyone like that, to take his own chances and that's the way it was.

speaker: HS
words: Like when we'd go up on the passenger train and tell the conductor we was

speaker: HS
words: just going to jump off up there, he says "be careful." And he'll usually

speaker: HS
words: come out when we'd jump off. The section crew would go up on the train in

speaker: HS
words: the wintertime and jump off into the snow someplace. They'd throw their

speaker: HS
words: stuff off and jump off.

speaker: ROB
words: When you were riding the freights did you run across any freight bums or

speaker: ROB
words: hobos?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, yes there'd be hobos here most all the time. They'd come in here and

speaker: HS
words: get something at the back door or something on the tracks. But I always

speaker: HS
words: figured a hobo, the reason he was a hobo is because he wasn't a crook. He

speaker: HS
words: was making an honest living. Because there was guys that steal but a hobo

speaker: HS
words: wouldn't steal much. It always makes me think of Purley Chaney up there at

speaker: HS
words: Troy. Come up there on day and "well" he says "I worked pretty slick on a

speaker: HS
words: guy this morning." He was always dressed up in old clothes. He done plumb

speaker: HS
words: ing and everything else. He says, "one come to my house the other day" and

speaker: HS
words: he says, "I saw him coming and I got outside before he could see that I

speaker: HS
words: came out of the house. And I just walked out and I passed him" and I says

speaker: HS
words: "no use trying here" he says, "no chance." So he says, "the other guy

speaker: HS
words: turned around too and he walked back." (Laugh.) But there used to be quite

speaker: HS
words: a few on the train, they didn't bother »em to much for awhile there. They'd

speaker: HS
words: be on there with their blankets, going back and forth and getting along.

speaker: HS
words: Cause those days each fellow took his own chance, you might say. You was

speaker: HS
words: more on your own. That railroad was quite an interesting thing. There was

speaker: HS
words: two trains, two passenger trains each way. One of »em a day train had a

speaker: HS
words: dining car on it, and the night train had a sleeper on it. And they'd pass

speaker: HS
words: at Juliaetta or here, and if they passed at Kendrick we used to get a return

speaker: HS
words: trip ticket to Juliaetta and ride up on one and catch the dfoher one back

speaker: HS
words: again, just for the ride. But that was one of the great pastimes on Sundays

speaker: HS
words: was to go meet the trains. The platform would just be full of people on

speaker: HS
words: Sundays when the train come in. See the train come in. See two of them

speaker: HS
words: there then wait for the next train and visitand that was their visiting

speaker: HS
words: place. And then of course those days the mail was distributed then. You

speaker: HS
words: got your mail afterwards. You had mail service in those days, but now we

speaker: HS
words: don't have mail service anymore. (Laugh.) Yah you could get your mail

speaker: HS
words: everyday of the week, holidays and all.

speaker: ROB
words: When were the bums or hobos around most on the railroads?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, I don't know. I can just remember back there when times were a little

speaker: HS
words: tough in there, about 1905 or 06 in there. I believe quite a lot of them

speaker: HS
words: were riding back and forth and take different trains. Now some of us fellows,

speaker: HS
words: freight train go along and weren't going too fast or something, to get some

speaker: HS
words: place we'd get on it. Even the passenger train. Another fellow and I one

speaker: HS
words: time got up here at Kendrick. Passenger train standing there. I wanted to

speaker: HS
words: go. Back of the cowcatcher there was kind of a platform up there. I says

speaker: HS
words: to the fellow "how about going up there, riding her down to Juliaetta."

speaker: HS
words: That's were he was going. I didn't more than say it and he was up there.

speaker: HS
words: I didn't back out and I went up too. And we set on that board. At Juliaetta

speaker: HS
words: there was a big crowd there watching the train come in, but the headlight

speaker: HS
words: blinded them all. They couldn't see us, didn't anyway. We got off and

speaker: HS
words: walked up and down and nobody every knew we was on there. But it was kinda

speaker: HS
words: funny riding. It seemed when you got to a curve the blame thing wasn't going

speaker: HS
words: to go around the curve. The front end just kept going out over the creek.

speaker: HS
words: (Laughs.) But we rode it down to Juliaettaa on the cowcatcher. I'll tell

speaker: HS
words: you those days you're more on your own. I don't know, you could take care

speaker: HS
words: of yourself more. For instance, you take there when I was a youngster. We

speaker: HS
words: had a shotgun and a twenty-two. If we wanted a few grouse or pheasant we'd

speaker: HS
words: go out and shoot a couple or three out there in the canyon. You didn't have

speaker: HS
words: any license or nothing. We'd shoot squirrels. If you wanted to go fishing

speaker: HS
words: you'd get your fishing poles and go down and get a few fish. You didn't

speaker: HS
words: need a license to do anything. For instance, even in 1920 Ed Deobald and

speaker: HS
words: I started a garage up here. We had the money drawer where the money was

speaker: HS
words: in...

speaker: HS
words: (End of Side B)

speaker: HS
words: Anyway money drawer in the office. We got a dollar or two and we put it

speaker: HS
words: in there, and when we had a little more money than we thought we oughta

speaker: HS
words: leave in the drawer we'd divide it out. Each one take half and put in his

speaker: HS
words: pocket. When we'd go over and eat at a restaurant, either one of us, why we'd

speaker: HS
words: just take a little money out of the drawer. We never kept track of anything.

speaker: HS
words: We'd haul gasoline from Pullman, we'd take a truck up there and bring down

speaker: HS
words: a bunch of 50 gallon barrels we had, sell out gas. The only thing we paid

speaker: HS
words: was taxes at the end of the year, property tax, and didn't worry about any

speaker: HS
words: thing else. Now I look at it...that was 1920 or '21, in those 50 years there

speaker: HS
words: was sure a bunch added on wasn't there? I didn't need no hunting license.

speaker: HS
words: It come in then, but they didn't bother you. I never had any those days.

speaker: HS
words: I never had any fishing license. We hunted and fished and everything else.

speaker: HS
words: What ever there was you just did it and that's the size of it. But any

speaker: HS
words: thing you do you kinda do it on your own. It's your own chances you was

speaker: HS
words: taking with what you're doing. Like riding a train or something like that,

speaker: HS
words: it's just more or less up to the chances you took.

speaker: ROB
words: What do you think started to change that?

speaker: HS
words: Well, progress I guess you might say. I think that'll about take it. It

speaker: HS
words: just kept getting going just a little more. You have to have this and if

speaker: HS
words: you're going to have the paved highway you're going to have to get some money

speaker: HS
words: someplace. We didn't have those, we had chuck holes in the streets up here.

speaker: HS
words: Ed Deobald and I, we put some signs out in the mud holes: No Duck Hunting

speaker: HS
words: Allowed. Just for a joke. About October, people take (their automobiles)

speaker: HS
words: home. Leave 'em home and put 'em up on blocks and take the battery out. He

speaker: HS
words: and I serviced the batteries and kept 'em charged through the wintertime.

speaker: HS
words: We'd have as high as 150 batteries in our shop there, just keep 'em charged.

speaker: HS
words: There was no cars running or anything else, but we'd overhaul some cars for

speaker: HS
words: people and rebuild batteries and work on cars and like that. It would usually

speaker: HS
words: be after Easter before it would be much going, unless it did freeze sometime

speaker: HS
words: and some would take the car out in winter. The sleds broke a pretty good

speaker: HS
words: track, but course they were narrower. Sometimes we'd go out, I know we used

speaker: HS
words: to go out and ride motorcycles. We used to go out in the winter and wrap a

speaker: HS
words: rope around for a chain and go in the snow.

speaker: ROB
words: Wrap a rope around the tire?

speaker: HS
words: Yeh, wrap a rope around a tire instead of a chain. Yeh, I had my first

speaker: HS
words: motorcycle in 1911. I had a Indian motorcycle. I bought it second hand.

speaker: HS
words: Single cyclinder it was. It had a brake in it, a coaster brake, but in order

speaker: HS
words: to start it you had to peddle it. So you either get on it and peddled it

speaker: HS
words: and kinda go downhill a little to start, or else run by the side of it. Then

speaker: HS
words: drop your valve, hold your valve open and twist your handle which would re56

speaker: HS
words: lease the valve, and then it would start and jump on and go. And then the

speaker: HS
words: next one I got was a twin- cylinder one. I went to Spokane and bought it.

speaker: HS
words: 1912 I think it was. A fellow took me outside of town up there and I come

speaker: HS
words: down the dusty roads. There was a piece of macadam in there around Waverly.

speaker: HS
words: Come through that way. But dusty up around Palouse, the roads were just

speaker: HS
words: dust. I think it was getting late in the fall and it just looked like it

speaker: HS
words: was raining right behind me. And I made it home. Got in the yard down at

speaker: HS
words: Juliaetta and I started up towards the house and kaplunk the thing quit right

speaker: HS
words: then. And I shoved a ways. It started to rain pretty soon and it wasn't

speaker: HS
words: no riding. But the crankshaft between the two flywheels broke on the motor

speaker: HS
words: cycle. I was lucky to get home after all I went through all the way there.

speaker: HS
words: Well I overhauled it then and didn't get to ride it until I think in the

speaker: HS
words: winter sometime.

speaker: ROB
words: Wouldn't there be a guarantee on it or something if you bought it new like

speaker: ROB
words: that?

speaker: HS
words: It was a second-hand one. The first one I think I paid eighty-five dollars

speaker: HS
words: for it and I sold it for that. And the next one I paid one hundred and

speaker: HS
words: twenty-five and I think I sold it for about one hundred, I believe it was

speaker: HS
words: then. And my brother got the automobile in 1915. He was working in the

speaker: HS
words: garage then and bought it between us. We bought a Maxwell, the first ones

speaker: HS
words: with headlights and a battery starter.

speaker: ROB
words: When you were a young man what was the social life around Kendrick like?

speaker: HS
words: Well, after school sometime on our way home some of the boys would stop

speaker: HS
words: and we'd play catch or play ball or something like that. But generally it

speaker: HS
words: was working. Farming or hoeing or something. There'd be a show in town for

speaker: HS
words: ten cents, occasionally we'd go see that, and later on there was some roller

speaker: HS
words: skating up here at Kendrick. We'd come up here and roller skate. It would

speaker: HS
words: mostly be go squirrel hunting or out on the outside, or go along the creek

speaker: HS
words: go fishing, or just monkey around the creek someplace and wait around. Or

speaker: HS
words: play ball. In the wintertime there'd be a bunch together and maybe play

speaker: HS
words: some card games and something like that. And course Sundays neighbors would

speaker: HS
words: get together, too. And coasting, if there was any snow on the ground. But

speaker: HS
words: after we got motorcycles, some other boys got motorcycles, so we used to go

speaker: HS
words: out Sunday mornings and go up on the hills someplace with motorcycles.

speaker: HS
words: Probably go until something happened to 'em and fix 'em up again during the

speaker: HS
words: week and ride 'em again the following Sunday. (Laugh.) But we used 'em

speaker: HS
words: on the telephone work a little until we got the car and we used it. Ue

speaker: HS
words: still had to use the team til...oh I don't think they discarded the team

speaker: HS
words: until 1920 or so. Then you'd have to hire livery rigs to take you out.

speaker: ROB
words: Did there used to be Saturday night dances here in Kendrick?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, they had dances. It seemed like country boys didn't get out very

speaker: HS
words: much to the dances. I didn't start to go to dances until later on when I

speaker: HS
words: got in the garage business here and one thing and another. But they'd have

speaker: HS
words: dances.

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of affairs were they?

speaker: HS
words: What?

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of dances were they?

speaker: HS
words: They had a dance in the Oddfellows Hall up here. And then in the skating

speaker: HS
words: rink down in Juliaetta. They usually had an orchestra, a home orchestra

speaker: HS
words: that played, and they'd have old-time pieces. Missouri Waltz, Three O'clock

speaker: HS
words: In The Morning, and some of those pieces. (Laugh.) They had regular dances,

speaker: HS
words: they wasn't the kind they step off now you know. The time has changed. If

words: was just what you'd give 'em. Now if somebody hit me up someplace or some

words: thing, I wouldn't give 'em any money but I'd take him in to a restaurant and

words: ask them what they had. It they had some stew or something I says, "give him

words: a lot of times they give 'em a little more too. You know. Course some of

speaker: HS
words: 'em was different, but the hobo bunch they just a bunch of people they just

speaker: HS
words: didn't have anything and times was hard now. People around home why it was

speaker: HS
words: just help the neighbors. I could remember the neighbors we had. The husband

speaker: HS
words: would go up in the timber up here by Bovill and he'd just get back home once

speaker: HS
words: or twice all winter. And this woman had two boys and a girl and they had a

words: little garden there. Well we'd give them apples and just lots of things

speaker: HS
words: like that. People would just give 'em to 'em and help 'em along. And one

speaker: HS
words: just helped the others of the people you knew. I could remember people back

words: since I worked for telephone with Porter, say that was back in 1910 it was,

speaker: HS
words: '10 or '11. Go out in the country and at the same time and try to collect

speaker: HS
words: up some of the bills people owed. And maybe a fellow owed us a dollar a

speaker: HS
words: month, maybe he owed 6 or 8, 10 dollars sometimes. If we was lucky he says,

speaker: HS
words: "well we could spare you a couple of dollars" and that would be the size of

speaker: HS
words: it. But they seemed like they always make it. They had the income once a

speaker: HS
words: year and they'd run out until their crops come in. It was a whole lot more

speaker: HS
words: sociability about people. If anyone was sick, why the neighbors would be

words: there and they'd bring in the eats and they'd help, you know. They wasn't

speaker: HS
words: left like they are now. Now if anyone's sick they really can't find anyone

speaker: HS
words: because they're on welfare, and it's just a hard proposition. There was

speaker: HS
words: people that would go out, but they are generally busy, what few there is.

speaker: HS
words: You just have to go to a rest home or someplace. It's alright for the ones

speaker: HS
words: that's got it or even if they don't have it now they get this welfare and

speaker: HS
words: they're usually taken care of. But those days there was no such thing.

speaker: HS
words: There was a poor farm up out of Moscow and they could go up on a poor farm.

words: Well, it was up north of Moscow. I say north, up along old electric rail

speaker: HS
words: road there aways out of town. It just used to be just the general name of

speaker: HS

words: that's where they are,just up against it. They take 'em up to the poor farm

speaker: HS
words: and the county would keep 'em going. But I don't know whether anyone got

speaker: HS
words: any county money, I don't think they did. If they had to ask for something

speaker: HS
words: they just had to go to the poor farm and work what they could and the rest

speaker: HS
words: of it. If they couldn't work why then they was taken care of. But nothing

speaker: HS
words: to brag on. Some person would run the poor farm, the farm and all I think

speaker: HS
words: if I remember right. They didn't have nice clean bed or stuff like they've

speaker: HS
words: got now, they just had to take care of their own. They'd have some blankets

speaker: HS
words: and stuff, I guess it was more up to them to keep them clean and one thing

speaker: HS
words: and another, and sleep in the bunkhouses and things. These younger generation

speaker: HS
words: they don't what it used to be for those people, even for the kids you know.

words: to ride on, the handlebars were broken off right close. There was two pegs

words: as well walk to town anyway. But riding a horse then wasn't the fun as

words: they take it to be now. We had to ride horses too much. It wasn't a treat.

words: all the way through. Why, on a hot day, 2 bits. (Laugh.)

words: it didn't take very many two bits to capitol your total assets. It didn't

words: take very much and you'd just have twice as much as you had before. I doubt

words: let me ride on the draper, on the platform coming home cause I wasn't able

speaker: HS
words: to walk that far. I think they paid a 150 dollars for it, on time, and paid

speaker: HS

speaker: HS
words: acre. It used to be my job if you run a lead horse or lead team was to drive

speaker: HS
words: the lead team. On our home place I'd ride the lead team. First, before I

speaker: HS
words: was able to do much, why whoever shocked I would be out there dragging the

speaker: HS
words: bundles up to »em for 'em, before I could lift 'em up around. A person was

speaker: HS
words: out there just as soon as he's able to do something, why you had a job. And

words: course it was bond and shocked, the hay those days, and then they stacked it.

words: Then run the thrashing machine inbetween two stacks and thrash off of the

words: stack. They didn't haul it in like they did later on. Sometimes it'd be

speaker: HS

words: ton. And when people come after hay why I'd go pitching hay, and usually

words: that. And apples and potatoes and stuff like that, we had our own and

words: gathered 'em up and had 'em put away.

words: Yes, we raise our oen and we even ground it. 1908 Otto and I was at the

words: around with some machinery before and we hooked this up on a corn sheller.

words: We had a grinder too that we could grind corn and grain, and we had it and

words: we'd usually raise oh maybe six, eight, ten acres of corn. Sometimes we'd

words: run the wagon out there through and just pull the corn ears out right out

speaker: HS
words: there and throw »em in the wagon, and the rest of the fodder let it go down.

words: wards and then shell it at a granary there. We'd shell it with this power

words: there and thrash it with horses. Just drive horses around it until it was

words: thrashed out, and fan it out with the breeze enough for chicken feed. But

words: kraut to make, and potatoes to dig, gather apples for the winter, go get

words: of gates and get »em in the barn and get 'em the feed if they was fed, milk

words: this cannery. There was a stock company. Well we didn't have any money to

words: buy in this stock with and so we couldn't buy any. So they told us the

words: Well, we had a job in the warehouse.

speaker: HS
words: Well, if they figured that we couldn't

speaker: HS
words: take it well we didn't. But they had that cannery down there and I was work

words: I was around with that guy and wanted to know if I won't come over and come

speaker: HS
words: down and see if I couldn't straighten 'em out. The cans leak, couldn't keep

speaker: HS

words: it adjusted so it wouldn't leak and I went back to the warehouse. I think

words: I spent the rest of the day there (at the cannery) keeping tab on it. The

words: capper going, that's all I had to do was to watch that going. Well, I'd

words: else. I worked there. That was good pay when 30 cents an hour at the ware

words: a pile and the others got on a pile and there was a chain went on by there

words: They would go

speaker: HS

words: about 2 minutes and get steamed up good so they wouldn't bush when they was

words: cooked. They go through that and come to the machine and go through the

words: put 'em and cook »em in hot water and cool 'em. But I remember the most

words: tomatoes. And get »em out. They also had a cannery in Lewiston. They had
